1 Chapter 1: Spatial Weights and Neighborhood Structures

The foundation of areal data modeling rests upon the precise mathematical representation of spatial relationships between geographic regions. Unlike geostatistical models that treat space as a continuous domain, areal models partition space into discrete regions‚ÄĒcensus tracts, counties, administrative boundaries‚ÄĒand model dependencies through carefully constructed neighborhood structures. This chapter develops the theoretical framework for spatial weight matrices, the primary mathematical tool for encoding spatial relationships in discrete spatial systems.

The construction of spatial weights requires translating geographic proximity into numerical relationships that preserve essential spatial structure while remaining computationally tractable. We establish rigorous definitions for various weight matrix constructions, analyze their mathematical properties, and demonstrate their implementation using Julia‚Äôs sparse matrix capabilities and graph-theoretic tools.

1.1 Fundamental Definitions and Notation

Consider a spatial system consisting of n areal units indexed by i = 1, 2, ‚Ä¶, n. Let S = {s‚āĀ, s‚āā, ‚Ä¶, s‚āô} denote the collection of spatial regions, where each sŠĶĘ represents a measurable subset of ‚ĄĚ¬≤ with well-defined boundaries.

Definition 1.1 (Spatial Weight Matrix). A spatial weight matrix W is an n √ó n matrix with elements wŠĶĘ‚Īľ representing the strength of spatial relationship between regions i and j. By convention, wŠĶĘŠĶĘ = 0 for all i, meaning regions do not neighbor themselves.

The spatial weight matrix encodes the neighborhood structure of the spatial system. Each element wŠĶĘ‚Īľ quantifies how strongly region j influences region i, with wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 0 indicating no direct spatial relationship.

Definition 1.2 (Neighborhood Set). For a given spatial weight matrix W, the neighborhood set of region i is defined as NŠĶĘ = {j : wŠĶĘ‚Īľ > 0}, representing all regions that directly neighbor region i.

The cardinality |NŠĶĘ| gives the number of neighbors for region i, which varies significantly across different weighting schemes and geographic configurations.

1.2 Contiguity-Based Weights

Contiguity weights represent the most intuitive approach to spatial relationships, defining neighbors as regions sharing common boundaries. This approach mirrors the fundamental geographic principle that proximate locations exhibit stronger spatial dependence.

1.2.1 Queen Contiguity

Queen contiguity defines two regions as neighbors if they share any boundary point, including corner connections. This corresponds to the 8-connectivity concept in digital image processing.

Definition 1.3 (Queen Contiguity). Regions i and j are queen neighbors if their closures intersect in a set with positive length: cl(sŠĶĘ) ‚ą© cl(s‚Īľ) contains a line segment or arc.

Queen contiguity typically produces denser weight matrices since corner adjacencies create additional connections. For regular lattice structures, interior regions have eight queen neighbors compared to four rook neighbors.

1.2.2 Rook Contiguity

Rook contiguity requires regions to share a boundary segment of positive length, corresponding to 4-connectivity in digital topology.

Definition 1.4 (Rook Contiguity). Regions i and j are rook neighbors if their boundaries intersect in a set with positive one-dimensional measure: ‚ąāsŠĶĘ ‚ą© ‚ąās‚Īľ contains a line segment or arc of positive length.

Rook contiguity produces sparser matrices but may better represent certain physical processes where spatial influence requires substantial boundary contact.

using SparseArrays, Graphs, DelimitedFiles, LinearAlgebra
using Plots, Colors, Random

# Function to construct queen contiguity matrix from polygon coordinates
function queen_contiguity(polygons::Vector{Vector{Tuple{Float64,Float64}}})
    n = length(polygons)
    W = spzeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in (i+1):n
            if are_queen_neighbors(polygons[i], polygons[j])
                W[i,j] = 1.0
                W[j,i] = 1.0  # Symmetric for contiguity
            end
        end
    end
    
    return W
end

# Helper function to check if two polygons are queen neighbors
function are_queen_neighbors(poly1, poly2; tol=1e-10)
    # Check if any vertex of poly1 is within tolerance of poly2 boundary
    for vertex in poly1
        if point_near_polygon(vertex, poly2, tol)
            return true
        end
    end
    
    # Check if any vertex of poly2 is within tolerance of poly1 boundary  
    for vertex in poly2
        if point_near_polygon(vertex, poly1, tol)
            return true
        end
    end
    
    return false
end

function point_near_polygon(point, polygon, tol)
    x, y = point
    n = length(polygon)
    
    # Check distance to each edge of polygon
    for i in 1:n
        j = (i == n) ? 1 : i + 1
        x1, y1 = polygon[i]
        x2, y2 = polygon[j]
        
        # Distance from point to line segment
        dist = point_to_segment_distance((x, y), (x1, y1), (x2, y2))
        if dist < tol
            return true
        end
    end
    
    return false
end

function point_to_segment_distance(point, seg_start, seg_end)
    x, y = point
    x1, y1 = seg_start  
    x2, y2 = seg_end
    
    # Vector from seg_start to seg_end
    dx = x2 - x1
    dy = y2 - y1
    
    if dx == 0 && dy == 0
        return sqrt((x - x1)^2 + (y - y1)^2)
    end
    
    # Parameter t for projection onto line segment
    t = ((x - x1) * dx + (y - y1) * dy) / (dx^2 + dy^2)
    t = clamp(t, 0.0, 1.0)
    
    # Closest point on segment
    closest_x = x1 + t * dx
    closest_y = y1 + t * dy
    
    return sqrt((x - closest_x)^2 + (y - closest_y)^2)
end

# Example: Create a 3x3 grid of square regions
function create_grid_polygons(rows, cols)
    polygons = Vector{Vector{Tuple{Float64,Float64}}}()
    
    for i in 1:rows
        for j in 1:cols
            # Square with corners at (j-1, i-1), (j, i-1), (j, i), (j-1, i)
            square = [(j-1.0, i-1.0), (j, i-1.0), (j, i), (j-1.0, i)]
            push!(polygons, square)
        end
    end
    
    return polygons
end

# Demonstrate queen contiguity on 3x3 grid
polygons_3x3 = create_grid_polygons(3, 3)
W_queen = queen_contiguity(polygons_3x3)

println("Queen contiguity matrix for 3√ó3 grid:")
display(Matrix(W_queen))

1.2.3 Boundary Length Weighted Contiguity

A refinement of binary contiguity weights proportionally weights neighbors by the length of shared boundary, providing more nuanced spatial relationships.

Definition 1.5 (Boundary Length Weights). For contiguous regions i and j, the boundary length weight is wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = ‚ĄďŠĶĘ‚Īľ/LŠĶĘ, where ‚ĄďŠĶĘ‚Īľ is the length of shared boundary between regions i and j, and LŠĶĘ is the total perimeter of region i.

This weighting scheme ensures that regions sharing longer boundaries have stronger connections, which often better reflects the underlying spatial processes.

1.3 Distance-Based Weights

Distance-based weights define spatial relationships through geometric distance, accommodating non-contiguous spatial systems and providing continuous measures of spatial proximity.

1.3.1 Inverse Distance Weights

Inverse distance weighting follows the fundamental geographic principle that spatial influence decays with distance.

Definition 1.6 (Inverse Distance Weights). For regions i and j with centroids at locations xŠĶĘ and x‚Īľ, the inverse distance weight is:

wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = { d(xŠĶĘ, x‚Īľ)‚ĀĽŠĶÖ if i ‚Č† j 0 if i = j }

where d(¬∑,¬∑) is a distance metric and őĪ > 0 is the distance decay parameter.

The parameter őĪ controls the rate of distance decay. Common choices include őĪ = 1 (inverse distance) and őĪ = 2 (inverse squared distance, following Newton‚Äôs law of gravitation).

# Function to construct inverse distance weight matrix
function inverse_distance_weights(centroids::Vector{Tuple{Float64,Float64}}, 
                                alpha=1.0, cutoff=Inf)
    n = length(centroids)
    W = spzeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            if i != j
                dist = euclidean_distance(centroids[i], centroids[j])
                if dist < cutoff && dist > 0
                    W[i,j] = dist^(-alpha)
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return W
end

function euclidean_distance(p1, p2)
    x1, y1 = p1
    x2, y2 = p2
    return sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2)
end

# Example: Centroids of 3x3 grid
centroids_3x3 = [(j-0.5, i-0.5) for i in 1:3 for j in 1:3]

# Inverse distance weights with cutoff
W_invdist = inverse_distance_weights(centroids_3x3, 1.0, 2.0)

println("Inverse distance weights (őĪ=1, cutoff=2.0):")
display(Matrix(W_invdist))

1.3.2 K-Nearest Neighbor Weights

K-nearest neighbor (KNN) weights ensure each region has exactly k neighbors, providing uniform connectivity across the spatial system.

Definition 1.7 (K-Nearest Neighbor Weights). For each region i, the k-nearest neighbor weight matrix assigns wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 1 if region j is among the k closest regions to region i, and wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 0 otherwise.

KNN weights create directed graphs since the k-nearest neighbor relationship is generally asymmetric: if j is among the k nearest neighbors of i, i may not be among the k nearest neighbors of j.

using NearestNeighbors

# Function to construct k-nearest neighbor weight matrix
function knn_weights(centroids::Vector{Tuple{Float64,Float64}}, k::Int)
    n = length(centroids)
    
    # Convert to matrix format for NearestNeighbors.jl
    coords = hcat([[p[1], p[2]] for p in centroids]...)
    kdtree = KDTree(coords)
    
    W = spzeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        # Find k+1 nearest neighbors (including self)
        indices, _ = knn(kdtree, coords[:, i], k + 1)
        
        # Remove self from neighbors
        neighbors = filter(j -> j != i, indices)
        
        # Take first k neighbors if we got more than k
        for j in neighbors[1:min(k, length(neighbors))]
            W[i, j] = 1.0
        end
    end
    
    return W
end

# Example: 4-nearest neighbors for 3x3 grid
W_knn = knn_weights(centroids_3x3, 4)

println("4-nearest neighbor weights:")
display(Matrix(W_knn))

1.4 Row Standardization

Raw spatial weights often exhibit substantial variation in row sums, reflecting differences in neighborhood sizes or distance configurations. Row standardization normalizes weights to facilitate model interpretation and estimation.

Definition 1.8 (Row Standardized Weights). Given a spatial weight matrix W, the row standardized matrix W* has elements:

wŠĶĘ‚Īľ* = { wŠĶĘ‚Īľ / ő£‚āĖ wŠĶĘ‚āĖ if ő£‚āĖ wŠĶĘ‚āĖ > 0 0 if ő£‚āĖ wŠĶĘ‚āĖ = 0 }

Row standardization ensures that ő£‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ* = 1 for all regions i with at least one neighbor, creating a row stochastic matrix.

Theorem 1.1 (Properties of Row Standardized Weights). Let W* be the row standardized version of a non-negative spatial weight matrix W. Then: 1. W* is row stochastic: each row sums to 1 (for connected regions) 2. The spectral radius ŌĀ(W) ‚Č§ 1 3. W has eigenvalue 1 with corresponding eigenvector proportional to the vector of row connectivities

Proof. Property 1 follows directly from the definition. For property 2, let őĽ be any eigenvalue of W* with eigenvector v. Then |őĽ||vŠĶĘ| = |ő£‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľv‚Īľ| ‚Č§ ő£‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ|v‚Īľ| ‚Č§ max_j |v‚Īľ| for any i, implying |őĽ| ‚Č§ 1. Property 3 holds since W*ūĚüô = ūĚüô where ūĚüô is the vector of ones (for connected regions). ‚Ė°

# Function to row standardize a weight matrix
function row_standardize(W)
    n = size(W, 1)
    W_std = copy(W)
    
    for i in 1:n
        row_sum = sum(W[i, :])
        if row_sum > 0
            W_std[i, :] ./= row_sum
        end
    end
    
    return W_std
end

# Example: Row standardize the queen contiguity matrix
W_queen_std = row_standardize(W_queen)

println("Row standardized queen contiguity matrix:")
display(Matrix(W_queen_std))

# Verify row sums
row_sums = [sum(W_queen_std[i, :]) for i in 1:size(W_queen_std, 1)]
println("Row sums: ", row_sums)

1.5 Mathematical Properties of Weight Matrices

Understanding the mathematical properties of spatial weight matrices is crucial for model specification and computational implementation.

1.5.1 Symmetry and Asymmetry

Definition 1.9 (Symmetric Weight Matrix). A spatial weight matrix W is symmetric if wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = w‚ĪľŠĶĘ for all i, j.

Contiguity-based weights are naturally symmetric, while KNN and certain economic weights are typically asymmetric. Symmetry affects eigenvalue structure and model interpretation significantly.

Lemma 1.2 (Eigenvalues of Symmetric Weights). If W is a symmetric spatial weight matrix, then all eigenvalues of W are real.

Proof. This follows from the spectral theorem for real symmetric matrices. ‚Ė°

1.5.2 Connectivity and Graph Properties

The connectivity structure of spatial weight matrices corresponds directly to graph-theoretic concepts, providing powerful analytical tools.

Definition 1.10 (Connected Weight Matrix). A spatial weight matrix W is connected if the corresponding graph G(W) = (V, E) with vertices V = {1, 2, ‚Ä¶, n} and edges E = {(i,j) : wŠĶĘ‚Īľ > 0} is connected.

Connectivity ensures that information can propagate throughout the spatial system, preventing the formation of isolated subregions.

using Graphs

# Function to check connectivity of weight matrix
function is_connected(W)
    # Create graph from weight matrix
    g = SimpleGraph(size(W, 1))
    
    rows, cols, _ = findnz(W)
    for (i, j) in zip(rows, cols)
        if i != j  # Exclude self-loops
            add_edge!(g, i, j)
        end
    end
    
    return is_connected(g)
end

# Function to find connected components
function connected_components_weights(W)
    g = SimpleGraph(size(W, 1))
    
    rows, cols, _ = findnz(W)
    for (i, j) in zip(rows, cols)
        if i != j
            add_edge!(g, i, j)
        end
    end
    
    return connected_components(g)
end

# Check connectivity of our example matrices
println("Queen contiguity connected: ", is_connected(W_queen))
println("Inverse distance connected: ", is_connected(W_invdist))
println("KNN connected: ", is_connected(W_knn))

# Find connected components
components_queen = connected_components_weights(W_queen)
println("Connected components (Queen): ", components_queen)

1.5.3 Eigenvalue Bounds

The eigenvalue structure of spatial weight matrices determines stability and convergence properties of spatial autoregressive models.

Theorem 1.3 (Gershgorin Circle Theorem for Spatial Weights). Let W be an n √ó n spatial weight matrix with eigenvalues őĽ‚āĀ, őĽ‚āā, ‚Ä¶, őĽ‚āô. Then each eigenvalue lies within at least one of the Gershgorin discs:

DŠĶĘ = {z ‚ąą ‚Ąā : |z - wŠĶĘŠĶĘ| ‚Č§ ő£‚Īľ‚Č†ŠĶĘ |wŠĶĘ‚Īľ|}

For spatial weights with wŠĶĘŠĶĘ = 0, this simplifies to |őĽ‚āĖ| ‚Č§ max_i ő£‚Īľ‚Č†ŠĶĘ |wŠĶĘ‚Īľ|.

This theorem provides bounds on eigenvalues essential for determining parameter spaces in spatial autoregressive models.

# Function to compute eigenvalue bounds using Gershgorin's theorem
function gershgorin_bounds(W)
    n = size(W, 1)
    bounds = zeros(n, 2)  # [lower, upper] bounds for each disc
    
    for i in 1:n
        center = W[i, i]  # Usually 0 for spatial weights
        radius = sum(abs.(W[i, :]) for j in 1:n if j != i)
        bounds[i, 1] = center - radius
        bounds[i, 2] = center + radius
    end
    
    overall_bound = maximum(bounds[:, 2])
    return bounds, overall_bound
end

# Compute actual eigenvalues for comparison
function compute_eigenvalues(W)
    return eigvals(Matrix(W))
end

# Example: Analyze eigenvalue bounds
bounds_queen, max_bound_queen = gershgorin_bounds(W_queen_std)
eigs_queen = compute_eigenvalues(W_queen_std)

println("Gershgorin bound: ", max_bound_queen)
println("Actual eigenvalue range: [", minimum(real.(eigs_queen)), ", ", 
        maximum(real.(eigs_queen)), "]")
println("Largest eigenvalue: ", maximum(real.(eigs_queen)))

1.6 Higher-Order Spatial Weights

Higher-order spatial relationships capture indirect neighborhood effects through paths of length greater than one in the spatial graph.

Definition 1.11 (k-th Order Spatial Weights). The k-th order spatial weight matrix W‚ĀĹŠĶŹ‚Āĺ has elements wŠĶĘ‚Īľ‚ĀĹŠĶŹ‚Āĺ equal to the number of paths of length exactly k from region i to region j in the spatial graph.

For binary adjacency matrices, W‚ĀĹŠĶŹ‚Āĺ = WŠĶŹ, where exponentiation is standard matrix multiplication.

Higher-order weights reveal extended spatial relationships and clustering patterns not apparent in first-order structures.

# Function to compute higher-order spatial weights
function higher_order_weights(W, k::Int)
    if k == 1
        return W
    else
        return W * higher_order_weights(W, k-1)
    end
end

# More efficient implementation using matrix power
function higher_order_weights_efficient(W, k::Int)
    return W^k
end

# Example: Compute second and third order weights
W_binary = (W_queen .> 0) .* 1.0  # Convert to binary
W_2nd = higher_order_weights_efficient(W_binary, 2)
W_3rd = higher_order_weights_efficient(W_binary, 3)

println("Second-order connections:")
display(Matrix(W_2nd))
println("\nThird-order connections:")  
display(Matrix(W_3rd))

# Analyze path structure
function analyze_path_structure(W, max_order=3)
    n = size(W, 1)
    path_counts = zeros(Int, n, n, max_order)
    
    W_binary = (W .> 0) .* 1.0
    
    for k in 1:max_order
        W_k = W_binary^k
        path_counts[:, :, k] = Int.(W_k)
    end
    
    return path_counts
end

paths = analyze_path_structure(W_queen, 3)
println("Paths of length 1 from region 1: ", paths[1, :, 1])
println("Paths of length 2 from region 1: ", paths[1, :, 2])
println("Paths of length 3 from region 1: ", paths[1, :, 3])

1.7 Economic and Interaction-Based Weights

Beyond purely geometric relationships, spatial weights can incorporate economic, social, or interaction-based measures of connectivity.

1.7.1 Trade-Based Weights

Economic spatial weights reflect the intensity of economic relationships between regions, such as trade flows, migration patterns, or financial linkages.

Definition 1.12 (Trade-Based Weights). Let tŠĶĘ‚Īľ represent the volume of trade (or other economic flow) from region i to region j. Trade-based weights are defined as:

wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = tŠĶĘ‚Īľ / ő£‚āĖ‚Č†ŠĶĘ tŠĶĘ‚āĖ

These weights naturally incorporate economic distance, which may differ substantially from geographic distance.

1.7.2 Interaction Potential Weights

Gravity-model inspired weights combine distance decay with economic mass measures.

Definition 1.13 (Gravity Weights). For regions with economic masses mŠĶĘ and m‚Īľ (e.g., GDP, population) and distance dŠĶĘ‚Īľ, gravity weights are:

wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = (mŠĶĘŠĶÖ m‚ĪľŠĶĚ) / dŠĶĘ‚ĪľŠĶě

where őĪ, ő≤, ő≥ > 0 are parameters calibrated to empirical interaction patterns.

# Function to construct gravity-based weights
function gravity_weights(masses::Vector{Float64}, 
                        centroids::Vector{Tuple{Float64,Float64}},
                        őĪ=1.0, ő≤=1.0, ő≥=2.0)
    n = length(masses)
    W = spzeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            if i != j
                dist = euclidean_distance(centroids[i], centroids[j])
                if dist > 0
                    W[i,j] = (masses[i]^őĪ * masses[j]^ő≤) / (dist^ő≥)
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return W
end

# Example: Create gravity weights with population-like masses
Random.seed!(123)
masses = rand(9) * 100  # Random masses between 0 and 100

W_gravity = gravity_weights(masses, centroids_3x3, 0.5, 0.5, 2.0)
W_gravity_std = row_standardize(W_gravity)

println("Gravity weights (row standardized):")
display(Matrix(W_gravity_std))

1.8 Computational Implementation with Sparse Matrices

Spatial weight matrices are typically sparse, especially for large geographic systems. Efficient implementation requires careful attention to sparse matrix structures and operations.

1.8.1 Sparse Matrix Construction

Julia‚Äôs SparseArrays.jl provides optimized data structures for sparse matrices, crucial for large-scale spatial systems.

using SparseArrays, BenchmarkTools

# Function to benchmark different weight matrix construction approaches
function benchmark_construction(n_regions::Int)
    # Generate random centroids
    centroids = [(rand(), rand()) for _ in 1:n_regions]
    
    println("Benchmarking for $n_regions regions:")
    
    # Dense construction (inefficient for large n)
    function dense_construction()
        W = zeros(n_regions, n_regions)
        for i in 1:n_regions
            for j in 1:n_regions
                if i != j
                    dist = euclidean_distance(centroids[i], centroids[j])
                    if dist < 0.3  # Cutoff distance
                        W[i,j] = 1/dist
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        return W
    end
    
    # Sparse construction (efficient)
    function sparse_construction()
        I = Int[]
        J = Int[]
        V = Float64[]
        
        for i in 1:n_regions
            for j in 1:n_regions
                if i != j
                    dist = euclidean_distance(centroids[i], centroids[j])
                    if dist < 0.3
                        push!(I, i)
                        push!(J, j)  
                        push!(V, 1/dist)
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        
        return sparse(I, J, V, n_regions, n_regions)
    end
    
    # Benchmark both approaches
    if n_regions <= 1000  # Only run dense for smaller problems
        println("Dense construction:")
        @btime dense_construction()
    end
    
    println("Sparse construction:")
    @btime sparse_construction()
    
    return sparse_construction()
end

# Demonstrate efficiency gains
W_small = benchmark_construction(100)
W_large = benchmark_construction(1000)

println("Small matrix sparsity: ", nnz(W_small) / (size(W_small,1)^2))
println("Large matrix sparsity: ", nnz(W_large) / (size(W_large,1)^2))

1.8.2 Graph-Theoretic Operations

The Graphs.jl package provides sophisticated algorithms for analyzing spatial weight matrices as graphs.

using Graphs, GraphPlot

# Function to convert weight matrix to graph and analyze properties
function analyze_spatial_graph(W)
    # Convert to simple graph (unweighted)
    n = size(W, 1)
    g = SimpleGraph(n)
    
    rows, cols, _ = findnz(W)
    for (i, j) in zip(rows, cols)
        if i < j  # Add each edge only once for undirected graph
            add_edge!(g, i, j)
        end
    end
    
    # Compute graph statistics
    stats = Dict(
        "vertices" => nv(g),
        "edges" => ne(g),
        "density" => density(g),
        "diameter" => is_connected(g) ? diameter(g) : Inf,
        "clustering" => global_clustering_coefficient(g),
        "components" => length(connected_components(g))
    )
    
    return g, stats
end

# Analyze our example matrices
g_queen, stats_queen = analyze_spatial_graph(W_queen)
g_knn, stats_knn = analyze_spatial_graph(W_knn)

println("Queen contiguity graph properties:")
for (key, val) in stats_queen
    println("  $key: $val")
end

println("\nKNN graph properties:")  
for (key, val) in stats_knn
    println("  $key: $val")
end

# Function to compute shortest path matrix
function spatial_shortest_paths(W)
    n = size(W, 1)
    g = SimpleGraph(n)
    
    rows, cols, _ = findnz(W)
    for (i, j) in zip(rows, cols)
        if i != j
            add_edge!(g, i, j)
        end
    end
    
    # Compute all-pairs shortest paths
    paths = zeros(Int, n, n)
    for i in 1:n
        dist = dijkstra_shortest_paths(g, i)
        paths[i, :] = dist.dists
    end
    
    return paths
end

# Compute shortest path distances
paths_queen = spatial_shortest_paths(W_queen)
println("\nShortest path distances (Queen contiguity):")
display(paths_queen)

1.9 Worked Examples

1.9.1 Example 1.1: Constructing Weights for Irregular Polygons

Consider a spatial system of four irregular administrative regions with known boundary coordinates. We construct both contiguity and distance-based weights, analyze their properties, and compare eigenvalue structures.

# Define four irregular regions as polygons
region_polygons = [
    [(0.0, 0.0), (2.0, 0.0), (2.5, 1.0), (1.5, 2.0), (0.0, 1.5)],  # Region 1
    [(2.0, 0.0), (4.0, 0.0), (4.0, 1.5), (2.5, 1.0)],              # Region 2  
    [(0.0, 1.5), (1.5, 2.0), (2.0, 3.0), (0.5, 3.0)],              # Region 3
    [(2.5, 1.0), (4.0, 1.5), (3.5, 3.0), (2.0, 3.0), (1.5, 2.0)]   # Region 4
]

# Compute centroids
function polygon_centroid(polygon)
    n = length(polygon)
    x_sum = sum(p[1] for p in polygon) / n
    y_sum = sum(p[2] for p in polygon) / n
    return (x_sum, y_sum)
end

centroids = [polygon_centroid(poly) for poly in region_polygons]
println("Region centroids: ", centroids)

# Construct different weight matrices
W_contiguity = queen_contiguity(region_polygons)
W_distance = inverse_distance_weights(centroids, 1.0, Inf)
W_knn_example = knn_weights(centroids, 2)

# Row standardize
W_contiguity_std = row_standardize(W_contiguity) 
W_distance_std = row_standardize(W_distance)
W_knn_std = row_standardize(W_knn_example)

# Compute and compare eigenvalues
eigs_contiguity = eigvals(Matrix(W_contiguity_std))
eigs_distance = eigvals(Matrix(W_distance_std))  
eigs_knn = eigvals(Matrix(W_knn_std))

println("\nEigenvalues comparison:")
println("Contiguity: ", sort(real.(eigs_contiguity), rev=true))
println("Distance:   ", sort(real.(eigs_distance), rev=true))
println("KNN:        ", sort(real.(eigs_knn), rev=true))

# Analyze connectivity patterns
println("\nConnectivity analysis:")
println("Contiguity neighbors per region: ", [sum(W_contiguity[i,:]) for i in 1:4])
println("Distance neighbors per region: ", [sum(W_distance .> 0)[i] for i in 1:4])
println("KNN neighbors per region: ", [sum(W_knn_example[i,:]) for i in 1:4])

1.9.2 Example 1.2: Economic Weight Construction

We construct trade-based weights using bilateral trade flow data and compare with distance-based weights to illustrate \newpage

2 Chapter 2: Spatial Autocorrelation Measures

Spatial autocorrelation quantifies the degree to which nearby observations on a spatial variable are correlated. Unlike temporal autocorrelation, where the ordering is natural, spatial autocorrelation requires explicit specification of neighborhood relationships through spatial weights matrices, as developed in Chapter 1. This chapter establishes the theoretical foundations of global and local spatial autocorrelation measures, their statistical properties, and computational implementation.

2.1 Global Measures of Spatial Autocorrelation

2.1.1 Moran‚Äôs I Statistic

The most widely used measure of spatial autocorrelation is Moran‚Äôs I, introduced by Patrick Moran in 1950. For a spatial variable observed on n areal units, Moran‚Äôs I provides a single summary statistic analogous to Pearson correlation but incorporating spatial structure.

Definition 2.1 (Moran‚Äôs I). Let y = (y‚āĀ, ‚Ä¶, y‚āô)ŠĶÄ be observations on n areal units with spatial weights matrix W = (wŠĶĘ‚Īľ). Moran‚Äôs I is defined as:

I = (n/S‚āÄ) ¬∑ (‚ąĎŠĶĘ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(yŠĶĘ - »≥)(y‚Īľ - »≥))/(‚ąĎŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ - »≥)¬≤)

where »≥ = n‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚ąĎŠĶĘyŠĶĘ and S‚āÄ = ‚ąĎŠĶĘ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ is the sum of all spatial weights.

The numerator measures covariation between spatially weighted observations and their deviations from the mean, while the denominator normalizes by the total variation. The factor n/S‚āÄ ensures comparability across different spatial weights specifications.

2.1.1.1 Statistical Properties of Moran‚Äôs I

Under the null hypothesis of no spatial autocorrelation, observations are independent and identically distributed. We distinguish two null hypothesis formulations:

  1. Randomization assumption: Observations are fixed, but their spatial arrangement is random.
  2. Normality assumption: Observations are independent normal random variables.

Theorem 2.1 (Expected Value of Moran‚Äôs I). Under the null hypothesis of no spatial autocorrelation, the expected value of Moran‚Äôs I is:

E[I] = -1/(n-1)

Proof. Under independence, E[(yŠĶĘ - »≥)(y‚Īľ - »≥)] = 0 for i ‚Č† j and E[(yŠĶĘ - »≥)¬≤] = ŌÉ¬≤(n-1)/n. The expected value follows from the structure of the spatial weights matrix and the constraint that ‚ąĎŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ - »≥) = 0. ‚Ė°

The variance of Moran‚Äôs I depends on the specific null hypothesis assumption:

Theorem 2.2 (Variance of Moran‚Äôs I - Randomization). Under the randomization assumption, the variance of Moran‚Äôs I is:

Var[I] = (n¬≤S‚āĀ - nS‚āā + 3S‚āÄ¬≤)/((n-1)(n-2)(n-3)S‚āÄ¬≤) - E[I]¬≤

where S‚āĀ = ¬Ĺ‚ąĎŠĶĘ‚ąĎ‚Īľ(wŠĶĘ‚Īľ + w‚ĪľŠĶĘ)¬≤ and S‚āā = ‚ąĎŠĶĘ(‚ąĎ‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľ + ‚ąĎ‚Īľw‚ĪľŠĶĘ)¬≤.

Theorem 2.3 (Variance of Moran‚Äôs I - Normality). Under the normality assumption, the variance simplifies to:

Var[I] = (nS‚āĀ - S‚āā + S‚āÄ¬≤)/((n-1)¬≤S‚āÄ¬≤)

The normality assumption yields a simpler expression and is often preferred for computational efficiency, though the randomization assumption makes fewer distributional assumptions.

2.1.1.2 Asymptotic Distribution and Hypothesis Testing

For large n, Moran‚Äôs I converges to a normal distribution under both null hypothesis assumptions:

Theorem 2.4 (Asymptotic Normality of Moran‚Äôs I). As n ‚Üí ‚ąě, under regularity conditions on the spatial weights matrix:

(I - E[I])/‚ąöVar[I] ‚Üí N(0,1)

This result enables construction of asymptotic tests. However, for moderate sample sizes, permutation tests often provide better finite-sample properties.

2.1.2 Implementation of Moran‚Äôs I

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays, Random, Distributions

function morans_i(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int})
    n = length(y)
    S‚āÄ = sum(W)
    
    # Center the data
    y_centered = y .- mean(y)
    
    # Compute numerator: spatial covariation
    numerator = dot(y_centered, W * y_centered)
    
    # Compute denominator: total variation
    denominator = sum(y_centered.^2)
    
    # Moran's I
    I = (n / S‚āÄ) * (numerator / denominator)
    
    return I
end

function morans_i_inference(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int}; 
                           assumption::Symbol=:randomization)
    n = length(y)
    I = morans_i(y, W)
    
    # Expected value under null
    E_I = -1 / (n - 1)
    
    # Variance calculation
    S‚āÄ = sum(W)
    W_sym = W + W'
    S‚āĀ = 0.5 * sum(W_sym.^2)
    
    row_sums = vec(sum(W, dims=2))
    col_sums = vec(sum(W, dims=1))
    S‚āā = sum((row_sums + col_sums).^2)
    
    if assumption == :randomization
        Var_I = (n^2 * S‚āĀ - n * S‚āā + 3 * S‚āÄ^2) / 
                ((n - 1) * (n - 2) * (n - 3) * S‚āÄ^2) - E_I^2
    elseif assumption == :normality
        Var_I = (n * S‚āĀ - S‚āā + S‚āÄ^2) / ((n - 1)^2 * S‚āÄ^2)
    else
        error("Assumption must be :randomization or :normality")
    end
    
    # Standardized test statistic
    z_score = (I - E_I) / sqrt(Var_I)
    p_value = 2 * (1 - cdf(Normal(), abs(z_score)))
    
    return (I=I, E_I=E_I, Var_I=Var_I, z_score=z_score, p_value=p_value)
end

# Permutation test for Moran's I
function morans_i_permutation_test(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int}; 
                                  n_permutations::Int=999)
    observed_I = morans_i(y, W)
    permuted_I = zeros(n_permutations)
    
    for i in 1:n_permutations
        y_perm = y[randperm(length(y))]
        permuted_I[i] = morans_i(y_perm, W)
    end
    
    # Two-tailed p-value
    p_value = (sum(abs.(permuted_I) .>= abs(observed_I)) + 1) / (n_permutations + 1)
    
    return (I=observed_I, permuted_I=permuted_I, p_value=p_value)
end

2.1.3 Geary‚Äôs C Statistic

While Moran‚Äôs I measures correlation-like association, Geary‚Äôs C focuses on differences between neighboring observations.

Definition 2.2 (Geary‚Äôs C). For observations y with spatial weights W, Geary‚Äôs C is defined as:

C = ((n-1)/2S‚āÄ) ¬∑ (‚ąĎŠĶĘ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(yŠĶĘ - y‚Īľ)¬≤)/(‚ąĎŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ - »≥)¬≤)

Geary‚Äôs C emphasizes local differences rather than global correlation patterns. Values near 1 indicate no spatial autocorrelation, values less than 1 indicate positive spatial autocorrelation, and values greater than 1 indicate negative spatial autocorrelation.

Theorem 2.5 (Expected Value of Geary‚Äôs C). Under the null hypothesis of no spatial autocorrelation:

E[C] = 1

function gearys_c(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int})
    n = length(y)
    S‚āÄ = sum(W)
    
    # Compute squared differences
    numerator = 0.0
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:n
            if W[i,j] > 0
                numerator += W[i,j] * (y[i] - y[j])^2
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Total variation
    denominator = sum((y .- mean(y)).^2)
    
    # Geary's C
    C = ((n - 1) / (2 * S‚āÄ)) * (numerator / denominator)
    
    return C
end

2.1.4 Example: Global Autocorrelation Analysis

Consider analyzing unemployment rates across U.S. counties. We demonstrate the computation and interpretation of global autocorrelation measures.

using Random
Random.seed!(123)

# Simulate county-level data
n_counties = 100
unemployment_rates = 5.0 .+ 2.0 * randn(n_counties)

# Create a simple spatial weights matrix (rook contiguity simulation)
function simulate_spatial_weights(n::Int)
    # Simple grid-based neighbors for demonstration
    W = spzeros(n, n)
    for i in 1:n
        # Add some neighboring relationships
        neighbors = sample(setdiff(1:n, i), min(4, n-1), replace=false)
        for j in neighbors
            W[i,j] = 1.0
        end
    end
    # Row-standardize
    row_sums = vec(sum(W, dims=2))
    for i in 1:n
        if row_sums[i] > 0
            W[i,:] ./= row_sums[i]
        end
    end
    return W
end

W = simulate_spatial_weights(n_counties)

# Compute Moran's I
results = morans_i_inference(unemployment_rates, W, assumption=:randomization)
println("Moran's I: $(round(results.I, digits=4))")
println("Expected I: $(round(results.E_I, digits=4))")
println("Z-score: $(round(results.z_score, digits=4))")
println("P-value: $(round(results.p_value, digits=4))")

# Permutation test
perm_results = morans_i_permutation_test(unemployment_rates, W, n_permutations=999)
println("Permutation p-value: $(round(perm_results.p_value, digits=4))")

# Compute Geary's C
C = gearys_c(unemployment_rates, W)
println("Geary's C: $(round(C, digits=4))")

2.2 Local Measures of Spatial Autocorrelation

Global measures like Moran‚Äôs I provide overall summaries but may obscure local patterns. Local indicators of spatial association (LISA) identify specific locations exhibiting significant spatial clustering or outlier behavior.

2.2.1 Local Moran‚Äôs I

Definition 2.3 (Local Moran‚Äôs I). For observation i, the local Moran‚Äôs I is:

IŠĶĘ = (yŠĶĘ - »≥)/ŌÉ¬≤ ¬∑ ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(y‚Īľ - »≥)

where ŌÉ¬≤ = n‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚ąĎŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ - »≥)¬≤.

Local Moran‚Äôs I decomposes the global statistic: ‚ąĎŠĶĘIŠĶĘ is proportional to global Moran‚Äôs I. Each IŠĶĘ measures the contribution of observation i to the global autocorrelation.

2.2.1.1 Interpretation of Local Moran‚Äôs I

The sign and magnitude of IŠĶĘ reveal local spatial patterns:

  • Positive IŠĶĘ: Location i and its neighbors have similar values (either both above or both below the mean)
  • Negative IŠĶĘ: Location i is a spatial outlier relative to its neighbors
  • Large |IŠĶĘ|: Strong local spatial association
  • Small |IŠĶĘ|: Weak local spatial association

2.2.2 Moran Scatterplot

The Moran scatterplot provides visual interpretation of spatial autocorrelation patterns by plotting standardized observations against their spatial lags.

Definition 2.4 (Spatial Lag). For observation yŠĶĘ with spatial weights wŠĶĘ‚Īľ, the spatial lag is:

(Wy)ŠĶĘ = ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľy‚Īľ

The Moran scatterplot plots (yŠĶĘ - »≥)/ŌÉ versus ‚ąĎ‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľ(y‚Īľ - »≥)/ŌÉ. The slope of the regression line equals Moran‚Äôs I. The four quadrants identify different spatial association patterns:

  1. High-High (HH): Above-average values surrounded by above-average neighbors
  2. Low-Low (LL): Below-average values surrounded by below-average neighbors
  3. High-Low (HL): Above-average values surrounded by below-average neighbors
  4. Low-High (LH): Below-average values surrounded by above-average neighbors

Quadrants 1 and 2 represent positive local spatial autocorrelation, while quadrants 3 and 4 represent negative local spatial autocorrelation (spatial outliers).

2.2.3 Local Moran‚Äôs I Implementation

function local_morans_i(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int})
    n = length(y)
    y_mean = mean(y)
    y_var = var(y, corrected=false)  # Population variance
    
    # Standardized values
    z = (y .- y_mean) / sqrt(y_var)
    
    # Spatial lag of standardized values
    W_z = W * z
    
    # Local Moran's I
    local_I = z .* W_z
    
    return local_I
end

function local_morans_i_inference(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int}; 
                                 n_permutations::Int=999)
    n = length(y)
    observed_local_I = local_morans_i(y, W)
    
    # Expected value under null (conditional on yŠĶĘ)
    E_local_I = zeros(n)  # All zeros under independence
    
    # Permutation test for each location
    p_values = zeros(n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        # Find neighbors of location i
        neighbors = findall(W[i,:] .> 0)
        if length(neighbors) == 0
            p_values[i] = NaN
            continue
        end
        
        # Permutation test
        count_extreme = 0
        for perm in 1:n_permutations
            # Permute neighbor values only
            y_perm = copy(y)
            y_perm[neighbors] = y_perm[neighbors[randperm(length(neighbors))]]
            
            local_I_perm = local_morans_i(y_perm, W)
            if abs(local_I_perm[i]) >= abs(observed_local_I[i])
                count_extreme += 1
            end
        end
        
        p_values[i] = (count_extreme + 1) / (n_permutations + 1)
    end
    
    return (local_I=observed_local_I, p_values=p_values)
end

# Moran scatterplot classification
function moran_scatterplot_quadrants(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int})
    n = length(y)
    y_mean = mean(y)
    
    # Standardized values and spatial lags
    z = y .- y_mean
    Wz = W * z
    
    # Classify quadrants
    quadrants = Vector{String}(undef, n)
    for i in 1:n
        if z[i] >= 0 && Wz[i] >= 0
            quadrants[i] = "HH"  # High-High
        elseif z[i] < 0 && Wz[i] < 0
            quadrants[i] = "LL"  # Low-Low
        elseif z[i] >= 0 && Wz[i] < 0
            quadrants[i] = "HL"  # High-Low
        else
            quadrants[i] = "LH"  # Low-High
        end
    end
    
    return (z=z, Wz=Wz, quadrants=quadrants)
end

2.2.4 Getis-Ord G and G* Statistics

The Getis-Ord statistics identify local hot spots and cold spots by focusing on the concentration of high or low values within local neighborhoods.

Definition 2.5 (Getis-Ord GŠĶĘ). For location i, the Getis-Ord GŠĶĘ statistic is:

GŠĶĘ = (‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľy‚Īľ)/(‚ąĎ‚Īľ y‚Īľ)

where the sum over j excludes location i itself.

**Definition 2.6 (Getis-Ord GŠĶĘ*).** The Getis-Ord GŠĶĘ* statistic includes location i in the calculation:

GŠĶĘ* = (‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ*y‚Īľ)/(‚ąĎ‚Īľ y‚Īľ)

where wŠĶĘŠĶĘ* = 1 and wŠĶĘ‚Īľ* = wŠĶĘ‚Īľ for i ‚Č† j.

The choice between GŠĶĘ and GŠĶĘ* depends on whether the focal location should be included in its own neighborhood definition. GŠĶĘ* is more commonly used as it avoids arbitrary exclusion of the focal observation.

2.2.4.1 Statistical Properties of G* Statistics

Under the null hypothesis of no spatial clustering, the standardized G* statistic follows approximately a standard normal distribution for large samples.

**Theorem 2.6 (Standardized GŠĶĘ*).** The standardized Getis-Ord statistic is:

Z(GŠĶĘ) = (GŠĶĘ - E[GŠĶĘ*])/‚ąöVar[GŠĶĘ*]

where E[GŠĶĘ*] = WŐĄŠĶĘ* and Var[GŠĶĘ*] involves moments of the spatial weights and observations.

function getis_ord_g_star(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int})
    n = length(y)
    y_sum = sum(y)
    
    # Create W* (include self-neighbors)
    W_star = copy(W)
    for i in 1:n
        W_star[i,i] = 1.0
    end
    
    # Compute G* for each location
    g_star = zeros(n)
    z_scores = zeros(n)
    
    for i in 1:n
        # Local sum including focal location
        local_sum = sum(W_star[i,j] * y[j] for j in 1:n)
        g_star[i] = local_sum / y_sum
        
        # Standardization (simplified version)
        # Expected value
        w_star_sum = sum(W_star[i,:])
        expected_g = w_star_sum / n
        
        # Variance calculation (approximate)
        y_mean = y_sum / n
        s_squared = sum((y .- y_mean).^2) / n
        variance_g = (w_star_sum * (n - w_star_sum) * s_squared) / 
                     ((n - 1) * y_mean^2)
        
        if variance_g > 0
            z_scores[i] = (g_star[i] - expected_g) / sqrt(variance_g)
        else
            z_scores[i] = 0.0
        end
    end
    
    # P-values (two-tailed)
    p_values = 2 .* (1 .- cdf.(Normal(), abs.(z_scores)))
    
    return (g_star=g_star, z_scores=z_scores, p_values=p_values)
end

2.2.5 Example: Local Spatial Analysis

# Local analysis of unemployment data
local_results = local_morans_i_inference(unemployment_rates, W, n_permutations=999)

println("Local Moran's I Analysis:")
significant_locations = findall(local_results.p_values .< 0.05)
println("Number of significant locations: $(length(significant_locations))")

# Moran scatterplot analysis
quadrant_results = moran_scatterplot_quadrants(unemployment_rates, W)
quadrant_counts = Dict()
for quad in ["HH", "LL", "HL", "LH"]
    quadrant_counts[quad] = count(quadrant_results.quadrants .== quad)
end
println("Quadrant counts: ", quadrant_counts)

# Getis-Ord G* analysis
getis_results = getis_ord_g_star(unemployment_rates, W)
hot_spots = findall((getis_results.z_scores .> 1.96) .& (getis_results.p_values .< 0.05))
cold_spots = findall((getis_results.z_scores .< -1.96) .& (getis_results.p_values .< 0.05))

println("Hot spots (high G*): $(length(hot_spots))")
println("Cold spots (low G*): $(length(cold_spots))")

2.3 Multiple Testing Correction

When conducting local spatial autocorrelation analysis, multiple hypothesis tests are performed simultaneously‚ÄĒone for each spatial unit. This multiplicity increases the probability of Type I errors (false discoveries). Multiple testing correction procedures help control the overall error rate.

2.3.1 The Multiple Testing Problem

If we conduct m independent hypothesis tests, each at significance level őĪ, the probability of making at least one Type I error is:

P(at least one Type I error) = 1 - (1-őĪ)^m

For m = 100 tests at őĪ = 0.05, this probability exceeds 0.99, making false discoveries nearly certain.

2.3.2 Bonferroni Correction

The most conservative approach is the Bonferroni correction, which controls the family-wise error rate (FWER).

Definition 2.7 (Bonferroni Correction). For m hypothesis tests, reject the null hypothesis for test i if pŠĶĘ ‚Č§ őĪ/m.

2.3.3 False Discovery Rate Control

The False Discovery Rate (FDR) approach, introduced by Benjamini and Hochberg (1995), provides less conservative control by limiting the expected proportion of false discoveries among rejected hypotheses.

Definition 2.8 (Benjamini-Hochberg Procedure). Order p-values as p‚āć‚āĀ‚āé ‚Č§ p‚āć‚āā‚āé ‚Č§ ‚Ä¶ ‚Č§ p‚āć‚āė‚āé. Find the largest k such that p‚āć‚āĖ‚āé ‚Č§ (k/m)őĪ. Reject hypotheses 1, 2, ‚Ä¶, k.

function multiple_testing_correction(p_values::Vector{Float64}; 
                                   method::Symbol=:fdr, alpha::Float64=0.05)
    m = length(p_values)
    
    if method == :bonferroni
        adjusted_alpha = alpha / m
        significant = p_values .<= adjusted_alpha
        return (significant=significant, adjusted_alpha=adjusted_alpha)
        
    elseif method == :fdr
        # Benjamini-Hochberg procedure
        sorted_indices = sortperm(p_values)
        sorted_p = p_values[sorted_indices]
        
        # Find largest k such that p(k) <= (k/m)*alpha
        k = 0
        for i in m:-1:1
            if sorted_p[i] <= (i/m) * alpha
                k = i
                break
            end
        end
        
        # Determine which hypotheses to reject
        significant = falses(m)
        if k > 0
            significant[sorted_indices[1:k]] .= true
        end
        
        return (significant=significant, threshold_index=k)
        
    else
        error("Method must be :bonferroni or :fdr")
    end
end

# Apply multiple testing correction to local analysis
corrected_local = multiple_testing_correction(local_results.p_values, method=:fdr)
corrected_getis = multiple_testing_correction(getis_results.p_values, method=:fdr)

println("Significant locations after FDR correction:")
println("Local Moran's I: $(sum(corrected_local.significant))")
println("Getis-Ord G*: $(sum(corrected_getis.significant))")

2.4 Simulation-Based Inference

In addition to analytical and permutation approaches, simulation-based inference provides flexibility for complex spatial autocorrelation testing scenarios.

2.4.1 Conditional Simulation

For testing local spatial autocorrelation, conditional simulation maintains the overall distribution of the data while randomizing spatial arrangements conditional on the observed values at non-focal locations.

function conditional_simulation_test(y::Vector{Float64}, W::SparseMatrixCSC{Float64,Int}, 
                                   focal_location::Int; n_simulations::Int=999)
    n = length(y)
    observed_local_I = local_morans_i(y, W)[focal_location]
    
    # Identify neighbors
    neighbors = findall(W[focal_location,:] .> 0)
    
    simulated_I = zeros(n_simulations)
    
    for sim in 1:n_simulations
        # Create simulation by fixing focal location, randomizing others
        y_sim = copy(y)
        non_focal = setdiff(1:n, focal_location)
        y_sim[non_focal] = y[non_focal[randperm(length(non_focal))]]
        
        simulated_I[sim] = local_morans_i(y_sim, W)[focal_location]
    end
    
    # Compute p-value
    p_value = (sum(abs.(simulated_I) .>= abs(observed_local_I)) + 1) / (n_simulations + 1)
    
    return (observed_I=observed_local_I, simulated_I=simulated_I, p_value=p_value)
end

2.5 Connections

The spatial autocorrelation measures developed in this chapter form the foundation for spatial regression modeling covered in Chapters 3-4. Moran‚Äôs I and related statistics help diagnose spatial dependence in regression residuals, motivating the need for spatial autoregressive and conditional autoregressive models. Local indicators guide the specification of spatially varying coefficient models (Chapter 5) by identifying regions where relationships may differ. The multiple testing framework extends naturally to multivariate settings (Chapter 6) and space-time contexts (Chapter 8), where the multiplicity problem becomes even more acute. These measures also connect to the geostatistical tradition from GEO-001, providing complementary perspectives on spatial dependence‚ÄĒglobal and local autocorrelation measures for areal data versus variogram analysis for point-referenced data.

2.6 Exercises

  1. Theoretical: Prove that the sum of local Moran‚Äôs I statistics is proportional to the global Moran‚Äôs I. Specifically, show that ‚ąĎŠĶĘIŠĶĘ = nI where I is the global Moran‚Äôs I.

  2. Computational: Implement a function to compute the exact variance of Moran‚Äôs I under the normality assumption. Compare analytical p-values with permutation test results for various spatial weights structures.

  3. Theoretical: Derive the expected value and variance of Geary‚Äôs C under the randomization assumption. Compare these moments with those of Moran‚Äôs I.

  4. Computational: Create a visualization function for the Moran scatterplot that colors points by quadrant and overlays the regression line. Apply this to simulated data with known spatial autocorrelation patterns.

  5. Applied: Using simulated data with spatial clusters, compare the power of local Moran‚Äôs I versus Getis-Ord G* statistics for detecting hot spots. Vary the cluster size and intensity.

  6. Computational: Implement the Benjamini-Yekutieli procedure for FDR control under dependence. This is more appropriate for spatial data where test statistics are correlated.

  7. Theoretical: For a spatial weights matrix representing a regular grid with rook contiguity, derive analytical expressions for S‚āÄ, S‚āĀ, and S‚āā in terms of the grid dimensions.

  8. Applied: Simulate data from a spatial autoregressive process and examine how global and local autocorrelation measures change with varying levels of spatial dependence. Plot the relationship between the true spatial parameter and estimated Moran‚Äôs I.

  9. Computational: Implement a bootstrap procedure for Moran‚Äôs I that resamples spatial units while preserving neighborhood structure. Compare bootstrap confidence intervals with analytical results.

  10. Advanced: Develop a multivariate extension of local Moran‚Äôs I for vector-valued observations. Test the procedure on simulated multivariate spatial data with cross-variable spatial dependence.

2.7 References

Anselin, L. (1995). Local indicators of spatial association‚ÄĒLISA. Geographical Analysis, 27(2), 93-115.

Benjamini, Y., & Hochberg, Y. (1995). Controlling the false discovery rate: A practical and powerful approach to multiple testing. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, 57(1), 289-300.

Cliff, A. D., & Ord, J. K. (1973). Spatial Autocorrelation. Pion Limited.

Geary, R. C. (1954). The contiguity ratio and statistical mapping. The Incorporated Statistician, 5(3), 115-146.

Getis, A., & Ord, J. K. (1992). The analysis of spatial association by use of distance statistics. Geographical Analysis, 24(3), 189-206.

Moran, P. A. P. (1950). Notes on continuous stochastic phenomena. Biometrika, 37(1/2), 17-23.

Ord, J. K., & Getis, A. (1995). Local spatial autocorrelation statistics: Distributional issues and an application. Geographical Analysis, 27(4), 286-306.

Sokal, R. R., & Oden, N. L. (1978). Spatial autocorrelation in biology: 1. Methodology. Biological Journal of the Linnean Society, 10(2), 199-228. \newpage

3 Chapter 3: Simultaneous Autoregressive (SAR) Models

Simultaneous autoregressive models provide the foundational framework for analyzing spatial dependence in areal data through explicit modeling of spatial interactions. Unlike the neighborhood-based conditional autoregressive models we will encounter in Chapter 4, SAR models specify simultaneous relationships among all spatial units, leading to rich theoretical properties and distinct computational challenges.

3.1 Spatial Lag Model (SLM)

The spatial lag model, also known as the spatial autoregressive model, directly incorporates spatially lagged dependent variables as explanatory factors. This specification naturally arises when spatial spillover effects are the primary mechanism generating spatial dependence.

Definition 3.1 (Spatial Lag Model). Let y = (y‚āĀ, y‚āā, ‚Ä¶, y‚āô)ŠĶÄ be observations on n spatial units, X be an n √ó k matrix of covariates, W be an n √ó n spatial weights matrix, and őĶ ~ N(0, ŌÉ¬≤I‚āô). The spatial lag model is given by:

y = ŌĀWy + Xő≤ + őĶ

where ŌĀ is the spatial autoregressive parameter and ő≤ is a k √ó 1 vector of regression coefficients.

The spatial lag model embodies the intuition that the outcome in region i depends not only on local characteristics XŠĶĘő≤ but also on outcomes in neighboring regions through the term ŌĀWy. The parameter ŌĀ quantifies the strength and direction of spatial spillover effects.

3.1.1 Reduced Form and Covariance Structure

To understand the implied covariance structure, we solve for the reduced form. Rearranging the SLM equation:

(I‚āô - ŌĀW)y = Xő≤ + őĶ

Assuming (I‚āô - ŌĀW) is invertible, the reduced form is:

y = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻXő≤ + (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ

Theorem 3.1 (SLM Covariance Structure). Under the spatial lag model with őĶ ~ N(0, ŌÉ¬≤I‚āô), the covariance matrix of y is:

Var(y) = ŌÉ¬≤[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)ŠĶÄ(I‚āô - ŌĀW)]‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

Proof. From the reduced form, y = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻXő≤ + (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ. Since E[őĶ] = 0 and Var(őĶ) = ŌÉ¬≤I‚āô:

Var(y) = Var[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ] = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻVar(őĶ)[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ]ŠĶÄ = ŌÉ¬≤(I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ]ŠĶÄ = ŌÉ¬≤[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)ŠĶÄ(I‚āô - ŌĀW)]‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚Ė°

This covariance structure reveals that spatial dependence in the SLM extends beyond immediate neighbors to all spatial units, with the strength of dependence decaying according to the spectral properties of W.

3.1.2 Identification Constraints

For the SLM to be well-defined, (I‚āô - ŌĀW) must be invertible for the relevant parameter space.

Theorem 3.2 (Invertibility Condition). Let őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô and őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď be the minimum and maximum eigenvalues of W. Then (I‚āô - ŌĀW) is invertible if and only if ŌĀ ‚ąČ {őĽŠĶĘ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ : i = 1, ‚Ä¶, n} where őĽŠĶĘ are the eigenvalues of W.

For row-standardized weights matrices, this constrains ŌĀ ‚ąą (1/őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô, 1) when őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô < 0, ensuring both mathematical tractability and economically meaningful spatial multipliers.

3.2 Spatial Error Model (SEM)

While the spatial lag model incorporates spatial dependence through lagged dependent variables, the spatial error model attributes spatial correlation to omitted spatially correlated variables captured in the error structure.

Definition 3.2 (Spatial Error Model). The spatial error model is specified as:

y = Xő≤ + u u = őĽWu + őĶ

where őĽ is the spatial error parameter, őĶ ~ N(0, ŌÉ¬≤I‚āô), and u represents spatially autocorrelated errors.

The SEM acknowledges that unobserved factors affecting the dependent variable may themselves exhibit spatial clustering. This specification is particularly appropriate when spatial dependence arises from omitted variables rather than direct spillover mechanisms.

3.2.1 Reduced Form of the Error Process

From the spatial error specification u = őĽWu + őĶ, we obtain:

u = (I‚āô - őĽW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ

Therefore, the SEM can be written as:

y = Xő≤ + (I‚āô - őĽW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ

Theorem 3.3 (SEM Covariance Structure). Under the spatial error model, the covariance matrix of y is:

Var(y) = ŌÉ¬≤[(I‚āô - őĽW)ŠĶÄ(I‚āô - őĽW)]‚ĀĽ¬Ļ

The proof follows directly from Theorem 3.1 by substituting őĽ for ŌĀ.

3.3 Spatial Durbin Model (SDM)

The spatial Durbin model generalizes the SLM by including spatially lagged covariates, allowing for spatial spillovers in both dependent and independent variables.

Definition 3.3 (Spatial Durbin Model). The spatial Durbin model is given by:

y = ŌĀWy + Xő≤ + WXőł + őĶ

where őł is a k √ó 1 vector of spatial lag coefficients for the covariates.

The SDM nests both the SLM (when őł = 0) and the spatial error model under the restriction őł = -ŌĀő≤. This nesting relationship makes the SDM a useful specification for testing between alternative spatial models.

3.3.1 Common Factor Restriction

Theorem 3.4 (SEM as Restricted SDM). The spatial error model is equivalent to the spatial Durbin model under the restriction őł = -őĽő≤.

Proof. Starting with the SEM: y = Xő≤ + (I‚āô - őĽW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ. Multiply both sides by (I‚āô - őĽW):

(I‚āô - őĽW)y = (I‚āô - őĽW)Xő≤ + őĶ

Expanding: y - őĽWy = Xő≤ - őĽWXő≤ + őĶ

Rearranging: y = őĽWy + Xő≤ - őĽWXő≤ + őĶ = őĽWy + Xő≤ + WX(-őĽő≤) + őĶ

This is the SDM with ŌĀ = őĽ and őł = -őĽő≤. ‚Ė°

This theorem provides the theoretical foundation for likelihood ratio tests comparing spatial models, as developed in the maximum likelihood estimation section below.

3.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation

Maximum likelihood estimation of SAR models requires careful treatment of the Jacobian determinant arising from the transformation y ‚Üí (I‚āô - ŌĀW)y.

3.4.1 Concentrated Log-Likelihood

For the spatial lag model y = ŌĀWy + Xő≤ + őĶ with őĶ ~ N(0, ŌÉ¬≤I‚āô), the log-likelihood function is:

ln L(ŌĀ, ő≤, ŌÉ¬≤) = -n/2 ln(2ŌÄ) - n/2 ln(ŌÉ¬≤) + ln|I‚āô - ŌĀW| - 1/(2ŌÉ¬≤)[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)y - Xő≤]ŠĶÄ[(I‚āô - ŌĀW)y - Xő≤]

Concentrating out ő≤ and ŌÉ¬≤, we obtain:

Definition 3.4 (Concentrated Log-Likelihood). The concentrated log-likelihood for the SLM is:

ln L_c(ŌĀ) = -n/2 ln(2ŌÄ) - n/2 + ln|I‚āô - ŌĀW| - n/2 ln[e(ŌĀ)ŠĶÄe(ŌĀ)/n]

where e(ŌĀ) = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)y - X(XŠĶÄX)‚ĀĽ¬ĻXŠĶÄ(I‚āô - ŌĀW)y represents the concentrated residuals.

3.4.2 Eigenvalue Computation

The key computational challenge lies in evaluating ln|I‚āô - ŌĀW| efficiently. For sparse weights matrices, direct computation of the determinant is prohibitive for large n.

Theorem 3.5 (Eigenvalue Decomposition of Log-Determinant). If W has eigenvalues őĽ‚āĀ, őĽ‚āā, ‚Ä¶, őĽ‚āô, then:

ln|I‚āô - ŌĀW| = ő£ŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ ln(1 - ŌĀőĽŠĶĘ)

This representation reduces the computational problem to eigenvalue calculation, which can be performed once and stored for the optimization routine.

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays, Optim, Distributions

function spatial_lag_mle(y, X, W; initial_ŌĀ=0.0)
    """
    Maximum likelihood estimation for spatial lag model
    y = ŌĀWy + Xő≤ + őĶ
    """
    n = length(y)
    
    # Compute eigenvalues of W for log-determinant
    eigenvals = eigvals(Matrix(W))
    
    # Precompute XŠĶÄX inverse for efficiency
    XtX_inv = inv(X' * X)
    
    function concentrated_loglik(ŌĀ)
        # Check parameter bounds
        if any(abs.(ŌĀ ./ eigenvals) .>= 1.0)
            return -Inf
        end
        
        # Compute log-determinant
        logdet_term = sum(log.(1.0 .- ŌĀ .* eigenvals))
        
        # Compute concentrated residuals
        Wy = W * y
        y_tilde = y - ŌĀ * Wy
        X_tilde = X - ŌĀ * (W * X)
        
        ő≤_hat = XtX_inv * (X' * y_tilde)
        residuals = y_tilde - X * ő≤_hat
        
        # Concentrated log-likelihood
        ssr = sum(residuals.^2)
        loglik = -0.5 * n * log(2ŌÄ) - 0.5 * n + logdet_term - 0.5 * n * log(ssr / n)
        
        return -loglik  # Return negative for minimization
    end
    
    # Optimize
    bounds = 1.0 ./ [maximum(eigenvals), minimum(eigenvals)]
    result = optimize(concentrated_loglik, bounds[2] + 1e-6, bounds[1] - 1e-6)
    
    ŌĀ_hat = result.minimizer
    
    # Compute final estimates
    Wy = W * y
    y_tilde = y - ŌĀ_hat * Wy
    ő≤_hat = XtX_inv * (X' * y_tilde)
    residuals = y_tilde - X * ő≤_hat
    ŌÉ¬≤_hat = sum(residuals.^2) / n
    
    return (ŌĀ = ŌĀ_hat, ő≤ = ő≤_hat, ŌÉ¬≤ = ŌÉ¬≤_hat, loglik = -result.minimum)
end

# Example: Simulate spatial lag data
Random.seed!(123)
n = 100
W = sprand(n, n, 0.05)  # Sparse random weights matrix
W = W ./ sum(W, dims=2)  # Row standardize

# Generate covariates and spatial lag data
X = [ones(n) randn(n, 2)]
ő≤_true = [1.0, 0.5, -0.3]
ŌĀ_true = 0.4
őĶ = randn(n)

# Solve for y: (I - ŌĀW)y = Xő≤ + őĶ
A = I - ŌĀ_true * W
y = A \ (X * ő≤_true + őĶ)

# Estimate model
results = spatial_lag_mle(y, X, W)
println("True ŌĀ: $(ŌĀ_true), Estimated ŌĀ: $(round(results.ŌĀ, digits=3))")
println("True ő≤: $(ő≤_true)")
println("Estimated ő≤: $(round.(results.ő≤, digits=3))")

3.5 Direct, Indirect, and Total Effects

A distinctive feature of spatial models is that marginal effects of covariates cannot be directly interpreted from coefficient estimates due to spatial feedback loops. LeSage and Pace (2009) developed the framework of direct, indirect, and total effects to properly interpret spatial spillovers.

3.5.1 Theoretical Framework

Consider the spatial lag model in reduced form: y = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻXő≤ + (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻőĶ

For a change in the k-th covariate by amount őĒ in region r, the total system response is: őĒy = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬ĻeŠĶ£őĒő≤‚āĖ

where eŠĶ£ is the r-th unit vector.

Definition 3.5 (Spatial Effects Decomposition). Let S(ŌĀ) = (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ. For covariate k:

  • Direct Effect: The average of diagonal elements of S(ŌĀ)ő≤‚āĖ
  • Indirect Effect: The average of row sums of off-diagonal elements of S(ŌĀ)ő≤‚āĖ
  • Total Effect: Direct Effect + Indirect Effect

3.5.2 Computational Implementation

For large spatial systems, computing (I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ explicitly is computationally intensive. We can use the power series expansion when |ŌĀ| < 1/őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď:

(I‚āô - ŌĀW)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = I‚āô + ŌĀW + ŌĀ¬≤W¬≤ + ŌĀ¬≥W¬≥ + ‚Ä¶

function compute_spatial_effects(ŌĀ, ő≤, W; max_iter=100, tol=1e-8)
    """
    Compute direct, indirect, and total effects using power series approximation
    """
    n = size(W, 1)
    k = length(ő≤)
    
    # Initialize power series terms
    S = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)  # S‚āÄ = I
    W_power = copy(W)             # W¬Ļ
    ŌĀ_power = ŌĀ                   # ŌĀ¬Ļ
    
    effects = Dict()
    
    for j in 1:k
        # Initialize effect matrices
        direct_matrix = ő≤[j] * Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)
        indirect_matrix = zeros(n, n)
        
        # Power series approximation
        for iter in 1:max_iter
            term = ŌĀ_power * ő≤[j] * W_power
            
            # Add to appropriate effect matrices
            direct_matrix += ő≤[j] * ŌĀ_power * Diagonal(diag(W_power))
            indirect_matrix += term - ő≤[j] * ŌĀ_power * Diagonal(diag(W_power))
            
            # Check convergence
            if maximum(abs.(term)) < tol
                break
            end
            
            # Update for next iteration
            W_power = W_power * W
            ŌĀ_power *= ŌĀ
        end
        
        # Compute summary effects
        direct_eff = mean(diag(direct_matrix))
        indirect_eff = mean(sum(indirect_matrix, dims=2))
        total_eff = direct_eff + indirect_eff
        
        effects["covariate_$j"] = (
            direct = direct_eff,
            indirect = indirect_eff, 
            total = total_eff
        )
    end
    
    return effects
end

# Example usage with previous simulation
effects = compute_spatial_effects(results.ŌĀ, results.ő≤[2:end], W)
for (var, eff) in effects
    println("$var:")
    println("  Direct: $(round(eff.direct, digits=4))")
    println("  Indirect: $(round(eff.indirect, digits=4))")
    println("  Total: $(round(eff.total, digits=4))")
end

3.5.3 Economic Interpretation

The effects decomposition reveals the mechanism through which spatial spillovers operate:

  1. Direct effects measure the impact of changing a covariate in region i on the outcome in region i, accounting for spatial feedback.

  2. Indirect effects capture the spillover impact on all other regions j ‚Č† i from changing the covariate in region i.

  3. Total effects represent the cumulative system-wide impact, including all rounds of spatial feedback.

These effects provide the proper tools for policy analysis in spatial settings, where interventions in one location generate ripple effects throughout the spatial system.

3.6 Bayesian SAR with Turing.jl

Bayesian estimation of SAR models offers several advantages: natural incorporation of prior information, uncertainty quantification for all parameters, and robust handling of identification issues through informative priors.

3.6.1 Prior Specification

For the spatial lag model y = ŌĀWy + Xő≤ + őĶ, we typically specify:

  • ŌĀ ~ Uniform(1/őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô, 1/őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď) to ensure invertibility
  • ő≤ ~ Normal(őľő≤, ő£ő≤) based on prior knowledge
  • ŌÉ¬≤ ~ InverseGamma(a, b) for conjugacy with normal likelihood
using Turing, MCMCChains, StatsPlots

@model function bayesian_sar(y, X, W, eigenvals)
    n, k = size(X)
    
    # Priors
    ŌĀ ~ Uniform(1/maximum(eigenvals) + 1e-6, 1/minimum(eigenvals) - 1e-6)
    ő≤ ~ MvNormal(zeros(k), 10.0 * I)
    ŌÉ¬≤ ~ InverseGamma(2.0, 1.0)
    
    # Log-determinant term
    logdet = sum(log.(1.0 .- ŌĀ .* eigenvals))
    
    # Likelihood
    őľ = (I - ŌĀ * W) * y - X * ő≤
    
    # Custom likelihood with Jacobian
    Turing.@addlogprob! logdet - n/2 * log(ŌÉ¬≤) - sum(őľ.^2) / (2*ŌÉ¬≤)
    
    return nothing
end

# Fit Bayesian SAR model
model = bayesian_sar(y, X, W, eigenvals)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(), 2000, progress=true)

# Examine results
println(chain)
plot(chain)

3.6.2 Model Comparison

Bayesian methods facilitate model comparison through information criteria and Bayes factors. The SDM‚Äôs nesting structure makes it particularly useful for model selection:

@model function bayesian_sdm(y, X, W, eigenvals)
    n, k = size(X)
    
    # Priors
    ŌĀ ~ Uniform(1/maximum(eigenvals) + 1e-6, 1/minimum(eigenvals) - 1e-6)
    ő≤ ~ MvNormal(zeros(k), 10.0 * I)
    őł ~ MvNormal(zeros(k), 10.0 * I)  # Spatial lag coefficients
    ŌÉ¬≤ ~ InverseGamma(2.0, 1.0)
    
    # Log-determinant
    logdet = sum(log.(1.0 .- ŌĀ .* eigenvals))
    
    # SDM likelihood
    WX = W * X
    őľ = (I - ŌĀ * W) * y - X * ő≤ - WX * őł
    
    Turing.@addlogprob! logdet - n/2 * log(ŌÉ¬≤) - sum(őľ.^2) / (2*ŌÉ¬≤)
    
    return nothing
end

# Compare models using WAIC
sdm_model = bayesian_sdm(y, X, W, eigenvals)
sdm_chain = sample(sdm_model, NUTS(), 2000)

# Model comparison would use packages like MLJTuring.jl or custom WAIC computation

3.7 Worked Example: Regional Employment Growth

Consider a regional science application examining employment growth spillovers among metropolitan statistical areas. Economic theory suggests that employment growth in one region may stimulate growth in neighboring regions through trade linkages, labor mobility, and knowledge spillovers.

Data Structure: - Dependent variable: Annual employment growth rate (%) - Covariates: Population growth, educational attainment, industrial diversity - Spatial weights: Based on inverse distance with 500km threshold

Model Specification: We compare three specifications: 1. OLS: yŠĶĘ = őĪ + ő≤‚āĀPopGrowthŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āāEducationŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āÉDiversityŠĶĘ + őĶŠĶĘ 2. SLM: yŠĶĘ = őĪ + ŌĀő£‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľy‚Īľ + ő≤‚āĀPopGrowthŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āāEducationŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āÉDiversityŠĶĘ + őĶŠĶĘ
3. SDM: yŠĶĘ = őĪ + ŌĀő£‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľy‚Īľ + ő≤‚āĀPopGrowthŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āāEducationŠĶĘ + ő≤‚āÉDiversityŠĶĘ + őł‚āĀő£‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľ(PopGrowth)‚Īľ + őł‚āāő£‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľ(Education)‚Īľ + őł‚āÉő£‚ĪľwŠĶĘ‚Īľ(Diversity)‚Īľ + őĶŠĶĘ

# Simulate regional employment data
using Random, SparseArrays, LinearAlgebra, Statistics

function simulate_regional_employment(n=200)
    Random.seed!(456)
    
    # Create distance-based weights matrix
    coords = rand(n, 2) * 1000  # Random coordinates in 1000km √ó 1000km region
    W = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        if i != j
            dist = sqrt(sum((coords[i,:] - coords[j,:]).^2))
            if dist <= 500  # 500km threshold
                W[i,j] = 1/dist
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Row standardize
    row_sums = sum(W, dims=2)
    W[row_sums .> 0, :] ./= row_sums[row_sums .> 0]
    
    # Generate covariates
    pop_growth = 2.0 .+ randn(n)      # Population growth rate
    education = 25.0 .+ 5*randn(n)    # % college educated
    diversity = 0.6 .+ 0.2*randn(n)   # Industrial diversity index
    
    X = [ones(n) pop_growth education diversity]
    
    # True parameters
    ő≤_true = [1.5, 0.3, 0.1, -0.5]
    ŌĀ_true = 0.25
    
    # Generate employment growth with spatial lag
    őĶ = randn(n) * 0.8
    employment_growth = (I - ŌĀ_true * sparse(W)) \ (X * ő≤_true + őĶ)
    
    return (
        y = employment_growth,
        X = X,
        W = sparse(W),
        coords = coords,
        covariates = (pop_growth=pop_growth, education=education, diversity=diversity)
    )
end

# Generate data and estimate models
data = simulate_regional_employment()
eigenvals = eigvals(Matrix(data.W))

# Estimate SAR models
ols_results = (X' * X) \ (X' * data.y)  # OLS for comparison
sar_results = spatial_lag_mle(data.y, data.X, data.W)

# Compute spatial effects
effects = compute_spatial_effects(sar_results.ŌĀ, sar_results.ő≤[2:end], data.W)

println("OLS Results:")
println("ő≤: $(round.(ols_results, digits=3))")
println("\nSAR Results:")
println("ŌĀ: $(round(sar_results.ŌĀ, digits=3))")  
println("ő≤: $(round.(sar_results.ő≤, digits=3))")
println("\nSpatial Effects:")
for (var, eff) in effects
    println("$var - Direct: $(round(eff.direct, digits=3)), Indirect: $(round(eff.indirect, digits=3)), Total: $(round(eff.total, digits=3))")
end

Interpretation: The spatial lag coefficient ŌĀ = 0.25 indicates significant positive employment growth spillovers. A 1% increase in employment growth in a region generates, on average, a 0.25% increase in spatially weighted neighboring employment growth. The effects decomposition reveals that population growth has both significant direct effects (0.3) and modest positive spillovers (indirect effects), while educational attainment shows primarily local benefits with limited spatial spillovers.

3.8 Exercises

  1. Theoretical Exercise: Prove that the spatial error model is equivalent to a spatial lag model with additional restrictions on the parameters. Specifically, show that y = Xő≤ + u with u = őĽWu + őĶ can be written as a constrained spatial Durbin model.

  2. Simulation Study: Implement a Monte Carlo simulation comparing the finite-sample properties of ML estimators for the SLM under different spatial weights specifications (rook contiguity vs. distance-based weights). Generate 1000 replications with n = 100, ŌĀ = 0.3, and examine bias, RMSE, and coverage probabilities.

  3. Computational Exercise: Implement the Ord eigenvalue method for computing log|I - ŌĀW| without explicit eigenvalue computation. Compare computational efficiency with the direct eigenvalue approach for sparse weights matrices of varying sizes (n = 100, 500, 1000).

  4. Effects Calculation: Extend the spatial effects computation to handle the spatial error model. Since SEM lacks direct spatial multipliers, develop an approach based on the reduced form covariance structure to quantify how shocks in one region propagate through the spatial system.

  5. Bayesian Implementation: Implement a Gibbs sampler for the spatial lag model using conjugate priors. Compare convergence properties and computational efficiency with the Hamiltonian Monte Carlo implementation in Turing.jl.

  6. Model Selection: Using the Bayesian framework, implement proper model comparison between SLM, SEM, and SDM specifications using the common factor restriction őł = -őĽő≤. Compute Bayes factors and posterior model probabilities for a simulated dataset.

  7. Applied Exercise: Analyze a real spatial dataset (e.g., county-level economic data) comparing non-spatial OLS, spatial lag, and spatial error models. Test for spatial autocorrelation in residuals, estimate spatial effects, and provide economic interpretation of the spillover mechanisms.

  8. Theoretical Extension: Derive the information matrix for the spatial lag model and prove asymptotic normality of the ML estimator. Show that the usual likelihood-based inference procedures (Wald, LR, and score tests) apply under regularity conditions.

  9. Computational Challenge: Implement the sparse matrix algorithms of Pace and Barry (1997) for computing spatial effects in large systems without inverting (I - ŌĀW). Test your implementation on a spatial system with n > 5000.

  10. Advanced Topic: Extend the SAR framework to handle spatial regimes or threshold effects, where spatial parameters vary across different regions or regimes. Implement both classical and Bayesian estimation approaches.

3.9 References

Anselin, L. (1988). Spatial Econometrics: Methods and Models. Kluwer Academic Publishers.

Elhorst, J. P. (2014). Spatial Econometrics: From Cross-Sectional Data to Spatial Panels. Springer.

LeSage, J. P., & Pace, R. K. (2009). Introduction to Spatial Econometrics. CRC Press.

Ord, K. (1975). Estimation methods for models of spatial interaction. Journal of the American Statistical Association, 70(349), 120-126.

Pace, R. K., & Barry, R. (1997). Quick computation of spatial autoregressive estimators. Geographical Analysis, 29(3), 232-247.

Wall, M. M. (2004). A close look at the spatial structure implied by the CAR and SAR models. Journal of Statistical Planning and Inference, 121(2), 311-324. \newpage

4 Chapter 4: Conditional Autoregressive (CAR) Models

Conditional autoregressive (CAR) models provide an alternative approach to modeling spatial dependence in areal data through the specification of conditional distributions for each region given its neighbors. Unlike the simultaneous autoregressive (SAR) models examined in Chapter 3, CAR models build the joint distribution from the bottom up by specifying how each observation depends on its spatial neighbors conditionally. This construction leads to different computational properties and modeling capabilities that make CAR models particularly attractive for Bayesian analysis and disease mapping applications.

The fundamental insight underlying CAR models is that spatial dependence can be naturally expressed through conditional relationships: the value at each location depends on the values at neighboring locations, with the strength of dependence determined by the spatial weights and model parameters. This conditional specification often aligns more naturally with the data generating processes we wish to model, particularly in epidemiological applications where disease rates in each region may be influenced by rates in neighboring regions through population movement, environmental factors, or other spatial spillover effects.

4.1 The Conditional Autoregressive Specification

The CAR model specifies the conditional distribution of the observation at each location given the values at all other locations. For a vector of observations y = (y‚āĀ, y‚āā, ‚Ä¶, y‚āô)‚Äô on n areal units, the CAR specification takes the form:

Definition 4.1 (Conditional Autoregressive Model). A random vector y follows a conditional autoregressive distribution if the conditional distribution of yŠĶĘ given all other observations y‚āčŠĶĘ = {y‚Īľ : j ‚Č† i} is

yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ ~ N(őľŠĶĘ + ŌĀ ‚ąĎ‚Īľ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(y‚Īľ - őľ‚Īľ), ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤)

where őľŠĶĘ is the mean for location i, ŌĀ is the spatial dependence parameter, wŠĶĘ‚Īľ are spatial weights with wŠĶĘŠĶĘ = 0, and ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤ is the conditional variance for location i.

The spatial weights matrix W = {wŠĶĘ‚Īľ} typically follows the same construction as in SAR models (Chapter 3), with wŠĶĘ‚Īľ > 0 when regions i and j are neighbors and wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 0 otherwise. However, the interpretation differs: in CAR models, wŠĶĘ‚Īľ determines how strongly observation j influences the conditional expectation of observation i.

4.1.1 Relationship to Markov Random Fields

The CAR specification defines a Markov random field (MRF) on the spatial graph defined by the neighborhood structure. The Markov property states that each observation depends only on its immediate neighbors, conditional on all other observations:

yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ = yŠĶĘ | y‚āô(ŠĶĘ)

where y‚āô(ŠĶĘ) denotes the values at locations neighboring i. This property underlies the computational advantages of CAR models, as it implies that the precision matrix (inverse covariance matrix) has zero entries corresponding to non-neighboring pairs of locations.

4.2 Brook‚Äôs Lemma and Joint Distributions

A fundamental challenge in working with CAR models is deriving the joint distribution from the conditional specifications. Brook‚Äôs lemma provides the mathematical foundation for this construction.

Theorem 4.1 (Brook‚Äôs Lemma). If the conditional distributions yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ have densities fŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ) and there exists a joint density f(y), then

f(y) ‚ąĚ ‚ąŹŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ fŠĶĘ(yŠĶĘ | y‚ĀĹŠĶĘ‚Āĺ)

where y‚ĀĹŠĶĘ‚Āĺ is any configuration such that y‚ĀĹŠĶĘ‚Āĺ‚Īľ = y‚Īľ for j ‚Č† i.

Proof. Consider the ratio f(y)/f(z) for two configurations y and z. By the chain rule:

f(y)/f(z) = ‚ąŹŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ [f(yŠĶĘ | y‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,yŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ,zŠĶĘ‚āä‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,z‚āô) / f(zŠĶĘ | y‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,yŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ,zŠĶĘ‚āä‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,z‚āô)]

Setting y‚ĀĹŠĶĘ‚Āĺ = (y‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,yŠĶĘ‚āč‚āĀ,zŠĶĘ‚āä‚āĀ,‚Ä¶,z‚āô) and using the conditional independence properties gives the result. ‚Ė°

Applying Brook‚Äôs lemma to the CAR specification with Gaussian conditional distributions yields important constraints on the model parameters.

Theorem 4.2 (CAR Joint Distribution). The CAR model with conditional distributions

yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ ~ N(őľŠĶĘ + ŌĀ ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(y‚Īľ - őľ‚Īľ), ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤)

has joint distribution y ~ N(őľ, ő£) where ő£‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = Q = D - ŌĀW with D diagonal matrix DŠĶĘŠĶĘ = 1/ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤, provided Q is positive definite.

Proof. From Brook‚Äôs lemma, the joint density is proportional to:

‚ąŹŠĶĘ‚āĆ‚āĀ‚ĀŅ exp{-1/(2ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤)[yŠĶĘ - őľŠĶĘ - ŌĀ ‚ąĎ‚Īľ wŠĶĘ‚Īľ(y‚Īľ - őľ‚Īľ)]¬≤}

Expanding the quadratic form and collecting terms in matrix notation gives:

f(y) ‚ąĚ exp{-¬Ĺ(y - őľ)‚ÄôQ(y - őľ)}

where Q = D - ŌĀW. For this to be a proper Gaussian distribution, Q must be positive definite. ‚Ė°

4.2.1 Constraints on Parameters

The requirement that Q = D - ŌĀW be positive definite places restrictions on the allowable values of ŌĀ. These constraints depend on the eigenvalues of D‚ĀĽ¬ĻW:

Theorem 4.3 (Parameter Constraints). For the CAR model to have a proper joint distribution, ŌĀ must satisfy

1/őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď(D‚ĀĽ¬ĻW) < ŌĀ < 1/őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô(D‚ĀĽ¬ĻW)

where őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď and őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô are the maximum and minimum eigenvalues of D‚ĀĽ¬ĻW.

When the conditional variances are equal (ŌÉŠĶĘ¬≤ = ŌÉ¬≤ for all i), this reduces to the familiar constraint from SAR models: 1/őĽ‚āė‚āź‚āď(W) < ŌĀ < 1/őĽ‚āėŠĶĘ‚āô(W).

4.3 Intrinsic Conditional Autoregressive Models

A particularly important special case arises when we consider the limit as certain eigenvalues approach the boundary of the parameter space, leading to singular precision matrices.

Definition 4.2 (Intrinsic CAR Model). The intrinsic conditional autoregressive (ICAR) model specifies

yŠĶĘ | y‚āčŠĶĘ ~ N(»≥.ŠĶĘ, ŌĄ¬≤/nŠĶĘ)

where »≥.ŠĶĘ = (1/nŠĶĘ)‚ąĎ‚Īľ~ŠĶĘ y‚Īľ is the mean of neighboring values and nŠĶĘ is the number of neighbors of location i.

The ICAR model corresponds to setting ŌĀ = 1 and using weights wŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 1/nŠĶĘ when j ~ i. The resulting precision matrix is:

Q = ŌĄ¬≤(D - W)

where DŠĶĘŠĶĘ = nŠĶĘ and WŠĶĘ‚Īľ = 1 when j ~ i. This matrix is singular, with null space spanned by the vector of ones 1. The ICAR model therefore defines an improper prior distribution that places equal probability on all spatial patterns that differ only by a constant.

4.3.1 Properties of the ICAR Model

The singularity of the ICAR precision matrix reflects an important property: the model is invariant to additive constants. If y follows an ICAR distribution, then so does y + c1 for any constant c. This invariance makes the ICAR model particularly suitable as a prior distribution for spatial random effects in hierarchical models.

The marginal variance structure of the ICAR model has been characterized by Rue and Held (2005):

Theorem 4.4 (ICAR Marginal Variances). For the ICAR model on a connected graph with precision matrix Q = ŌĄ¬≤(D - W), the generalized variance Var(yŠĶĘ - y‚Īľ) = (2/ŌĄ¬≤)RŠĶĘ‚Īľ where R = Q‚Āļ is the Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse of Q.

This result shows that the ICAR model induces different marginal variances at different locations, with the variance structure determined by the graph topology. Locations that are more central in the spatial network tend to have smaller marginal variances.

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays, Graphs, GraphMakie, CairoMakie
using Distributions, Random

"""
Construct ICAR precision matrix for a spatial graph.
"""
function icar_precision(adj_matrix::AbstractMatrix)
    n = size(adj_matrix, 1)
    D = Diagonal([sum(adj_matrix[i, :]) for i in 1:n])
    Q = D - adj_matrix
    return Q
end

"""
Sample from ICAR distribution with sum-to-zero constraint.
"""
function sample_icar(Q::AbstractMatrix, n_samples::Int = 1)
    n = size(Q, 1)
    # Add small ridge for numerical stability
    Q_ridge = Q + 1e-6 * I
    
    # Sample from N(0, Q_ridge^-1)
