        h = norm(coords_point[i] - coords_point[j])
        ő£[i,j] = ŌÉ¬≤ * exp(-h/ŌĀ)
    end
    
    # Point-to-area covariances (simplified)
    for i in 1:n_point, j in 1:n_areal
        # Use centroid approximation
        area_centroid = centroid(areas_areal[j])
        h = norm(coords_point[i] - area_centroid)
        ő£[i, n_point+j] = ő£[n_point+j, i] = ŌÉ¬≤ * exp(-h/ŌĀ)
    end
    
    # Area-to-area covariances (simplified using centroids)
    for i in 1:n_areal, j in 1:n_areal
        centroid_i = centroid(areas_areal[i])
        centroid_j = centroid(areas_areal[j])
        h = norm(centroid_i - centroid_j)
        ő£[n_point+i, n_point+j] = ŌÉ¬≤ * exp(-h/ŌĀ)
    end
    
    # Latent field
    z ~ MvNormal(zeros(n_total), ő£)
    
    # Data models
    for i in 1:n_point
        y_point[i] ~ Normal(z[i], sqrt(ŌĄ¬≤_p))
    end
    
    for j in 1:n_areal
        y_areal[j] ~ Normal(z[n_point + j], sqrt(ŌĄ¬≤_a))
    end
    
    return z
end

# Simulate example data
n_point = 20
n_areal = 10
n_pred = 15

# Generate synthetic data
coords_point = [rand(2) * 10 for _ in 1:n_point]
areas_areal = [Box(Point(rand()*8, rand()*8), Point(rand()*8+2, rand()*8+2)) for _ in 1:n_areal]

# True process values
z_true_point = [5 + 2*sin(p[1]) + cos(p[2]) + 0.5*randn() for p in coords_point]
z_true_areal = [5 + 2*sin(centroid(area)[1]) + cos(centroid(area)[2]) + 0.5*randn() for area in areas_areal]

# Observations with noise
y_point = z_true_point + 0.3*randn(n_point)
y_areal = z_true_areal + 0.2*randn(n_areal)

# Fit fusion model
model = spatial_data_fusion(y_point, y_areal, coords_point, areas_areal, n_pred)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(0.8), 1000)

# Extract predictions
z_samples = group(chain, :z)
z_mean = [mean(z_samples[:z][i]) for i in 1:(n_point+n_areal+n_pred)]

5.9.3 7.5.3 Bayesian Melding

Bayesian melding provides a formal framework for combining multiple sources of information about the same underlying process. Consider two models for the same process:

  • Model A: \(Y_A | \theta_A \sim f_A(y_A | \theta_A)\)
  • Model B: \(Y_B | \theta_B \sim f_B(y_B | \theta_B)\)

where both models make predictions about a common quantity \(\Delta = g_A(\theta_A) = g_B(\theta_B)\).

The melding approach introduces a pooling density \(\pi(\Delta)\) and constructs the joint posterior:

\[\pi(\theta_A, \theta_B | Y_A, Y_B) \propto \pi(\theta_A) \pi(\theta_B) f_A(Y_A | \theta_A) f_B(Y_B | \theta_B) \pi(\Delta)\]

subject to the constraint \(g_A(\theta_A) = g_B(\theta_B) = \Delta\).

5.10 7.6 Applications to Small Area Estimation

Small area estimation combines model-based and design-based inference to produce estimates for domains with insufficient sample sizes. The change of support perspective is crucial when auxiliary information is available at different spatial scales.

5.10.1 7.6.1 Fay-Herriot Model Extensions

The Fay-Herriot model for small area estimation can be extended to account for spatial correlation and mixed supports:

\[Y_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \boldsymbol{\beta} + v_i + e_i\]

where \(v_i \sim N(0, A)\) are area effects following a spatial process, and \(e_i \sim N(0, D_i)\) are sampling errors.

When auxiliary information \(\mathbf{x}_i\) is available at different spatial scales, data fusion techniques enable borrowing strength across scales:

using Turing, LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

@model function spatial_fay_herriot(y, X, W, D, n_areas)
    # Fixed effects
    ő≤ ~ MvNormal(zeros(size(X,2)), 10.0 * I)
    
    # Spatial variance components
    ŌÉ¬≤_v ~ InverseGamma(2, 1)
    ŌĀ ~ Gamma(2, 0.5)
    
    # Spatial precision matrix (CAR model)
    # W is the adjacency matrix
    Q = sparse(Diagonal(sum(W, dims=2)[:]) - ŌĀ * W)
    
    # Area effects
    v ~ MvNormal(zeros(n_areas), ŌÉ¬≤_v * inv(Matrix(Q)))
    
    # Likelihood
    őľ = X * ő≤ + v
    for i in 1:n_areas
        y[i] ~ Normal(őľ[i], sqrt(D[i]))
    end
end

# Example: Combine survey estimates with administrative data
n_areas = 50
p = 3

# Design matrix (mix of area-level and point-referenced covariates)
X = randn(n_areas, p)

# Adjacency matrix (simplified)
W = sparse(rand(0:1, n_areas, n_areas))
W = (W + W') / 2
W[diagind(W)] .= 0

# Sampling variances (known)
D = rand(0.1:0.01:0.5, n_areas)

# Synthetic direct estimates
ő≤_true = [2.0, -1.5, 0.8]
v_true = 0.5 * randn(n_areas)
y = X * ő≤_true + v_true + sqrt.(D) .* randn(n_areas)

# Fit model
model = spatial_fay_herriot(y, X, W, D, n_areas)
chain = sample(model, NUTS(0.8), 2000)

# Model-based estimates
ő≤_est = mean(group(chain, :ő≤))
println("Coefficient estimates: ", ő≤_est)

5.11 7.7 Climate Model Downscaling

Climate model downscaling represents a canonical change of support problem in environmental science. Global Climate Models (GCMs) produce outputs at coarse spatial resolutions (typically 100-200 km), while impact assessments require fine-scale information.

5.11.1 7.7.1 Statistical Downscaling Framework

Statistical downscaling establishes empirical relationships between large-scale climate predictors and local-scale weather variables. The spatial process model takes the form:

\[Y(s) = \sum_{k=1}^K \beta_k(s) X_k + Z(s) + \epsilon(s)\]

where: - \(Y(s)\) is the local weather variable (e.g., precipitation) - \(X_k\) are large-scale predictors (e.g., pressure patterns) - \(\beta_k(s)\) are spatially-varying coefficients - \(Z(s)\) is a residual spatial process - \(\epsilon(s)\) is measurement error

The coefficients \(\beta_k(s)\) are modeled as spatial processes themselves:

\[\beta_k(s) \sim \text{GP}(\mu_{\beta_k}, C_{\beta_k}(h))\]

5.11.2 7.7.2 Hierarchical Implementation

using Turing, GaussianProcesses

@model function climate_downscaling(y_obs, x_large, coords_fine, coords_coarse, n_pred)
    # Number of large-scale predictors
    K = size(x_large, 2)
    n_obs = length(y_obs)
    
    # Hyperparameters for coefficient processes
    ŌÉ¬≤_ő≤ ~ filldist(InverseGamma(2, 1), K)
    ŌĀ_ő≤ ~ filldist(Gamma(2, 1), K)
    őľ_ő≤ ~ MvNormal(zeros(K), 2.0 * I)
    
    #
\\newpage

# Chapter 8: Space-Time Areal Models

The extension of areal data models to the temporal dimension introduces fundamental challenges in modeling the complex interactions between spatial and temporal dependencies. While purely spatial models, as examined in Chapters 3-6, capture cross-sectional spatial autocorrelation, space-time models must simultaneously account for temporal evolution and spatial contagion effects. This dual dependency structure creates rich modeling possibilities but also substantial computational and inferential complexities.

Space-time areal models find critical applications across diverse domains. Disease surveillance systems rely on these models to detect emerging outbreaks by monitoring unusual spatio-temporal patterns. Crime analysts use them to predict hotspot locations and timing for resource allocation. Political scientists employ space-time models to understand how voting patterns evolve and diffuse across geographic regions. Economic forecasters apply these methods to predict regional unemployment rates and housing market dynamics.

The theoretical foundation builds upon the conditional autoregressive (CAR) and simultaneous autoregressive (SAR) frameworks from Chapters 3-4, extending them to incorporate temporal dynamics. The key innovation lies in specifying how spatial and temporal effects interact‚ÄĒwhether they operate independently (separable models) or exhibit complex interdependencies (non-separable models).

## Fundamental Framework for Space-Time Areal Models

Consider areal data observed over *n* spatial units and *T* time periods, yielding observations **y**_{it} for *i* = 1, ..., *n* and *t* = 1, ..., *T*. Let **Y** denote the *nT* √ó 1 vector stacking all observations, typically ordered as **Y** = (**y**_1^‚ä§, **y**_2^‚ä§, ..., **y**_T^‚ä§)^‚ä§ where **y**_t = (*y*_{1t}, *y*_{2t}, ..., *y*_{nt})^‚ä§.

The general space-time areal model takes the form:

**Y** = **őľ** + **őĶ**

where **őľ** represents the mean structure incorporating covariates and **őĶ** captures the space-time dependency structure. The fundamental challenge lies in specifying the covariance structure Cov(**őĶ**) = **ő£**_{ST} that appropriately models both spatial and temporal autocorrelation.

> **Definition 8.1 (Separable Space-Time Process).** A space-time process {*Y*_{it} : *i* = 1, ..., *n*, *t* = 1, ..., *T*} is separable if its covariance function can be written as the product of purely spatial and purely temporal components:
> 
> Cov(*Y*_{it}, *Y*_{js}) = ŌÉ¬≤*C*_S(*i*, *j*)*C*_T(*t*, *s*)
> 
> where *C*_S(*i*, *j*) depends only on spatial relationships and *C*_T(*t*, *s*) depends only on temporal separation.

Separability greatly simplifies computation and interpretation but imposes strong restrictions on the space-time interaction structure. Non-separable models allow for more complex interactions where spatial correlation patterns can change over time, or temporal dynamics can vary across space.

## Knorr-Held Space-Time Interaction Types

Knorr-Held (2000) proposed a systematic classification of space-time interactions in areal models, identifying four fundamental types that capture different mechanisms of spatio-temporal evolution. This taxonomy has become the standard framework for specifying space-time CAR models.

Let **W** denote the *n* √ó *n* spatial adjacency matrix and define temporal adjacencies where *őī*_{ts} = 1 if |*t* - *s*| = 1 and 0 otherwise. The four interaction types are:

> **Definition 8.2 (Knorr-Held Interaction Types).**
> 
> **Type I (No Interaction):** Spatial and temporal effects operate independently:
> *E*[*Y*_{it} | **Y**_{-it}] = *őĪ*_i + *ő≤*_t + *ŌÜ* ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} *Y*_{jt} + *ŌĀ* ‚ąĎ_{s : őī_{ts}=1} *Y*_{is}
> 
> **Type II (Spatial Main Effects, Temporal Interaction):** Spatial effects remain constant but temporal correlation varies by spatial location:
> *E*[*Y*_{it} | **Y**_{-it}] = *őĪ*_i + *ő≤*_t + *ŌÜ* ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} *Y*_{jt} + ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} ‚ąĎ_{s : őī_{ts}=1} *őł*_{ij} *Y*_{js}
> 
> **Type III (Temporal Main Effects, Spatial Interaction):** Temporal effects remain constant but spatial correlation evolves over time:
> *E*[*Y*_{it} | **Y**_{-it}] = *őĪ*_i + *ő≤*_t + ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} *ŌÜ*_t *Y*_{jt} + *ŌĀ* ‚ąĎ_{s : őī_{ts}=1} *Y*_{is}
> 
> **Type IV (Full Interaction):** Both spatial and temporal effects vary across space and time:
> *E*[*Y*_{it} | **Y**_{-it}] = *őĪ*_i + *ő≤*_t + ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} ‚ąĎ_{s : őī_{ts}=1} *Ōą*_{ijts} *Y*_{js}

### Type I Models: Independence

Type I models assume spatial and temporal autocorrelations operate through separate mechanisms. The precision matrix has Kronecker structure **Q** = **Q**_T ‚äó **Q**_S, where **Q**_S and **Q**_T are the spatial and temporal precision matrices respectively.

For the intrinsic CAR specification:
**Q**_S = *ŌĄ*_S (**D**_S - *ŌĀ*_S **W**)
**Q**_T = *ŌĄ*_T (**D**_T - *ŌĀ*_T **W**_T)

where **D**_S and **D**_T are degree matrices for spatial and temporal adjacencies, and **W**_T represents temporal adjacency (typically first-order random walk structure).

### Type II Models: Spatial-Temporal Interaction

Type II models allow temporal evolution to vary across spatial locations while maintaining consistent spatial structure. This captures phenomena like disease outbreaks that spread differently in urban versus rural areas, or economic shocks that propagate at different rates across regions.

The conditional mean structure becomes:
*E*[*Y*_{it} | **Y**_{-it}] = *őľ*_{it} + *ŌĀ*_S ‚ąĎ_{j ‚ąľ i} (*Y*_{jt} - *őľ*_{jt}) + *ŌĀ*_{Ti} ‚ąĎ_{s : őī_{ts}=1} (*Y*_{is} - *őľ*_{is})

where *ŌĀ*_{Ti} allows temporal correlation to vary by spatial unit *i*.

### Type III Models: Temporal-Spatial Interaction

Type III models maintain consistent temporal evolution while allowing spatial correlation patterns to change over time. This structure captures scenarios like political polarization where geographic clustering of similar political views strengthens or weakens over election cycles.

### Type IV Models: Full Interaction

Type IV models represent the most flexible specification, allowing all pairwise space-time interactions. While theoretically appealing, these models suffer from over-parameterization and require strong regularization or dimension reduction techniques for practical application.

## Dynamic Linear Models for Areal Data

Dynamic linear models (DLMs) provide a state-space framework for modeling time-evolving spatial patterns. The approach separates the observation equation, linking data to underlying states, from the state equation governing temporal evolution.

> **Definition 8.3 (Spatial Dynamic Linear Model).** A spatial DLM consists of:
> 
> **Observation Equation:** **y**_t = **F**_t **őł**_t + **v**_t, **v**_t ‚ąľ N(**0**, **V**_t)
> 
> **State Equation:** **őł**_t = **G**_t **őł**_{t-1} + **w**_t, **w**_t ‚ąľ N(**0**, **W**_t)
> 
> where **őł**_t represents the *p*-dimensional spatial state vector at time *t*, **F**_t is the *n* √ó *p* observation matrix, **G**_t is the *p* √ó *p* state evolution matrix, and **V**_t, **W**_t are covariance matrices for observation and state errors.

The spatial structure enters through the state vector **őł**_t, which can represent spatially correlated random effects, or through spatial covariances in **V**_t and **W**_t.

### Spatially Structured State Evolution

Consider a state vector **őł**_t = (**őł**_{1t}, ..., **őł**_{nt})^‚ä§ representing area-specific random effects. The state equation can incorporate spatial dependence:

**őł**_t = *ŌĀ* **W** **őł**_{t-1} + **w**_t

where **W** is the spatial weights matrix and **w**_t ‚ąľ N(**0**, *ŌÉ*_w¬≤ **I**). This specification allows temporal evolution to depend on spatially lagged values from the previous time period.

### Kalman Filter for Spatial DLMs

The Kalman filter provides optimal recursive estimation for linear Gaussian DLMs. For spatial applications, the key computational challenge lies in handling large covariance matrices when *n* is substantial.

The filter recursion becomes:
**Prediction:** **őł**_{t|t-1} = **G**_t **őł**_{t-1|t-1}, **P**_{t|t-1} = **G**_t **P**_{t-1|t-1} **G**_t^‚ä§ + **W**_t
**Update:** **őł**_{t|t} = **őł**_{t|t-1} + **K**_t (**y**_t - **F**_t **őł**_{t|t-1})

where **K**_t = **P**_{t|t-1} **F**_t^‚ä§ (**F**_t **P**_{t|t-1} **F**_t^‚ä§ + **V**_t)^{-1} is the Kalman gain.

```julia
using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, SparseArrays

function spatial_kalman_filter(Y, F, G, W_matrices, V_matrices, őł‚āÄ, P‚āÄ)
    """
    Kalman filter for spatial dynamic linear model
    
    Args:
        Y: n√óT matrix of observations
        F: n√óp observation matrix (assumed constant)
        G: p√óp state transition matrix (assumed constant) 
        W_matrices: Vector of p√óp state error covariance matrices
        V_matrices: Vector of n√ón observation error covariance matrices
        őł‚āÄ: p√ó1 initial state mean
        P‚āÄ: p√óp initial state covariance
    
    Returns:
        őł_filtered: p√óT matrix of filtered state estimates
        P_filtered: Vector of p√óp filtered covariance matrices
        loglik: Log-likelihood
    """
    n, T = size(Y)
    p = length(őł‚āÄ)
    
    # Storage for results
    őł_filtered = zeros(p, T)
    P_filtered = Vector{Matrix{Float64}}(undef, T)
    loglik = 0.0
    
    # Initialize
    őł_pred = copy(őł‚āÄ)
    P_pred = copy(P‚āÄ)
    
    for t in 1:T
        # Prediction error and covariance
        y_pred = F * őł_pred
        S = F * P_pred * F' + V_matrices[t]
        e = Y[:, t] - y_pred
        
        # Update log-likelihood
        loglik += -0.5 * (n * log(2ŌÄ) + logdet(S) + e' * (S \ e))
        
        # Kalman gain and update
        K = P_pred * F' / S
        őł_filtered[:, t] = őł_pred + K * e
        P_filtered[t] = (I - K * F) * P_pred
        
        # Predict next state (if not last time point)
        if t < T
            őł_pred = G * őł_filtered[:, t]
            P_pred = G * P_filtered[t] * G' + W_matrices[t+1]
        end
    end
    
    return őł_filtered, P_filtered, loglik
end

# Example: Disease surveillance model
function disease_surveillance_dlm(cases, population, W_spatial)
    """
    Dynamic linear model for disease surveillance with spatial random effects
    
    Args:
        cases: n√óT matrix of case counts
        population: n√óT matrix of population at risk
        W_spatial: n√ón spatial weights matrix
    """
    n, T = size(cases)
    
    # Log relative risk model: log(őĽŠĶĘ‚āú) = őłŠĶĘ‚āú + log(EŠĶĘ‚āú)
    # where EŠĶĘ‚āú = population[i,t] * overall_rate
    overall_rate = sum(cases) / sum(population)
    E = population .* overall_rate
    
    # Observation equation: cases ~ Poisson(E * exp(őł))
    # Approximate with Gaussian: log(cases/E) ‚Čą őł + error
    Y = log.(max.(cases ./ E, 0.01))  # Avoid log(0)
    
    # Observation matrix (identity for direct observation)
    F = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)
    
    # State evolution with spatial dependence
    ŌĀ = 0.7  # Temporal persistence  
    őĪ = 0.3  # Spatial dependence
    G = ŌĀ * I + őĪ * W_spatial
    
    # Error covariances
    ŌÉ_w¬≤ = 0.1
    ŌÉ_v¬≤ = 0.2
    W_matrices = [ŌÉ_w¬≤ * I for _ in 1:T]
    V_matrices = [ŌÉ_v¬≤ * I for _ in 1:T]
    
    # Initial conditions
    őł‚āÄ = zeros(n)
    P‚āÄ = Matrix{Float64}(I, n, n)
    
    # Run Kalman filter
    őł_filtered, P_filtered, loglik = spatial_kalman_filter(
        Y, F, G, W_matrices, V_matrices, őł‚āÄ, P‚āÄ)
    
    return őł_filtered, P_filtered, loglik
end

5.12 Autoregressive Space-Time Models

Autoregressive space-time models explicitly model the temporal evolution of spatial patterns through lagged dependencies. These models naturally extend the CAR framework to incorporate temporal dynamics while maintaining computational tractability.

5.12.1 First-Order Space-Time Autoregressive Model

The first-order space-time autoregressive model (STAR(1)) specifies:

y_t = ŌĀ W y_t + ŌÜ y_{t-1} + X_t ő≤ + őĶ_t

where ŌĀ captures spatial dependence, ŌÜ represents temporal persistence, X_t contains covariates, and őĶ_t ‚ąľ N(0, ŌÉ¬≤ I).

This model can be rewritten in reduced form as: (I - ŌĀ W) y_t = ŌÜ y_{t-1} + X_t ő≤ + őĶ_t

The temporal evolution respects spatial constraints, with each area‚Äôs value depending on both its previous value and the current values of neighboring areas.

5.12.2 Higher-Order Temporal Dependencies

Higher-order models incorporate multiple temporal lags:

y_t = ŌĀ W y_t + ‚ąĎ{k=1}^p ŌÜ_k y{t-k} + X_t ő≤ + őĶ_t

The order p determines the temporal memory of the system. Model selection criteria like AIC or BIC help determine appropriate lag structures.

5.12.3 Maximum Likelihood Estimation

For Gaussian errors, the log-likelihood for the STAR(1) model is:

‚Ąď(őł) = -nT/2 log(2ŌÄ) - T/2 log(ŌÉ¬≤) + T log|I - ŌĀ W| - 1/(2ŌÉ¬≤) ‚ąĎ_{t=1}^T e_t^‚ä§ e_t

where e_t = (I - ŌĀ W) y_t - ŌÜ y_{t-1} - X_t ő≤.

The Jacobian term T log|I - ŌĀ W| arises from the spatial autoregressive structure and requires careful computation for large n.

using Optim, ForwardDiff, SparseArrays

function star_loglikelihood(params, Y, X, W, n, T)
    """
    Log-likelihood for STAR(1) model
    
    Args:
        params: [ŌĀ, ŌÜ, ő≤..., log_ŌÉ¬≤]
        Y: n√óT matrix of observations  
        X: n√óT√ók array of covariates
        W: n√ón spatial weights matrix
        n, T: dimensions
    
    Returns:
        Negative log-likelihood
    """
    ŌĀ, ŌÜ = params[1:2]
    k = size(X, 3)
    ő≤ = params[3:(2+k)]
    log_ŌÉ¬≤ = params[end]
    ŌÉ¬≤ = exp(log_ŌÉ¬≤)
    
    # Check stationarity constraints
    if abs(ŌĀ) >= 1.0 || abs(ŌÜ) >= 1.0
        return Inf
    end
    
    # Spatial transformation matrix
    S = I - ŌĀ * W
    
    # Compute log determinant (expensive for large n)
    log_det_S = logdet(S)
    
    # Sum of squared errors
    sse = 0.0
    for t in 2:T  # Start from t=2 due to lag
        # Compute residual
        Xy_beta = sum(X[:, t, j] * ő≤[j] for j in 1:k)
        e = S * Y[:, t] - ŌÜ * Y[:, t-1] - Xy_beta
        sse += dot(e, e)
    end
    
    # Log-likelihood (negative for minimization)
    T_eff = T - 1  # Effective sample size after first lag
    loglik = -0.5 * (n * T_eff * log(2ŌÄ) + T_eff * log(ŌÉ¬≤) - T_eff * log_det_S + sse / ŌÉ¬≤)
    
    return -loglik
end

function fit_star_model(Y, X, W)
    """
    Maximum likelihood estimation for STAR(1) model
    
    Args:
        Y: n√óT matrix of observations
        X: n√óT√ók array of covariates  
        W: n√ón spatial weights matrix
    
    Returns:
        Fitted parameters and standard errors
    """
    n, T = size(Y)
    k = size(X, 3)
    
    # Initial parameter values
    ŌĀ‚āÄ = 0.1
    ŌÜ‚āÄ = 0.5  
    ő≤‚āÄ = zeros(k)
    log_ŌÉ¬≤‚āÄ = log(1.0)
    params‚āÄ = [ŌĀ‚āÄ; ŌÜ‚āÄ; ő≤‚āÄ; log_ŌÉ¬≤‚āÄ]
    
    # Objective function
    objective(params) = star_loglikelihood(params, Y, X, W, n, T)
    
    # Optimize
    result = optimize(objective, params‚āÄ, BFGS(), 
                     Optim.Options(show_trace=true, iterations=1000))
    
    # Extract results
    params_mle = Optim.minimizer(result)
    
    # Compute standard errors using Hessian
    hessian = ForwardDiff.hessian(objective, params_mle)
    std_errors = sqrt.(diag(inv(hessian)))
    
    return (
        ŌĀ = params_mle[1],
        ŌÜ = params_mle[2], 
        ő≤ = params_mle[3:(2+k)],
        ŌÉ¬≤ = exp(params_mle[end]),
        std_errors = std_errors,
        loglik = -Optim.minimum(result),
        convergence = Optim.converged(result)
    )
end

5.13 Separable versus Non-Separable Space-Time Structures

The distinction between separable and non-separable space-time structures fundamentally affects both model flexibility and computational complexity. This section examines the theoretical foundations and practical implications of each approach.

5.13.1 Mathematical Characterization of Separability

Theorem 8.1 (Kronecker Structure of Separable Models). A zero-mean Gaussian space-time process Y with covariance matrix ő£ is separable if and only if ő£ = ő£_T ‚äó ő£_S for some temporal covariance matrix ő£_T and spatial covariance matrix ő£_S.

Proof. The forward direction follows directly from the definition. For the reverse direction, suppose ő£ has Kronecker structure. Then Cov(Y_{it}, Y_{js}) = [ő£_T ‚äó ő£_S]{(i-1)T+t,(j-1)T+s} = [ő£_T]{ts} [ő£_S]_{ij}, establishing separability. ‚Ė°

This structure has profound computational implications. For separable models, the precision matrix Q = ő£^{-1} = Q_T ‚äó Q_S, enabling efficient algorithms that exploit Kronecker structure.

5.13.2 Computational Advantages of Separability

Separable models enjoy several computational advantages:

  1. Determinant Computation: log|ő£| = T log|ő£_S| + n log|ő£_T|
  2. Matrix Inversion: ő£^{-1} = ő£_T^{-1} ‚äó ő£_S^{-1}
  3. Linear System Solution: (ő£_T ‚äó ő£_S) x = b can be solved via matrix equations
  4. Eigendecomposition: Eigenvalues are products of temporal and spatial eigenvalues

These properties reduce computational complexity from O((nT)¬≥) to O(n¬≥ + T¬≥), making separable models feasible for large space-time datasets.

5.13.3 Non-Separable Extensions

Non-separable models capture richer space-time interactions at increased computational cost. Common approaches include:

Space-Time Mat√©rn Covariances: C(h, u) = ŌÉ¬≤ 2^{1-őĹ}/őď(őĹ) (‚ąö{h¬≤/őĪ_s¬≤ + u¬≤/őĪ_t¬≤})^őĹ K_őĹ(‚ąö{h¬≤/őĪ_s¬≤ + u¬≤/őĪ_t¬≤})

where h represents spatial separation, u temporal separation, and K_őĹ is the modified Bessel function.

Interaction Models: C(h, u) = ŌÉ¬≤ C_S(h) C_T(u) + ŌÉ_I¬≤ C_{ST}(h, u)

where C_{ST}(h, u) captures non-separable interactions.

5.13.4 Example: Crime Forecasting with Separable Models

using Kronecker, LinearAlgebra, Distributions

function separable_spacetime_car(Y, W_spatial, W_temporal, ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t, ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t)
    """
    Fit separable space-time CAR model using Kronecker structure
    
    Args:
        Y: n√óT matrix of observations (vectorized as nT√ó1)
        W_spatial: n√ón spatial adjacency matrix
        W_temporal: T√óT temporal adjacency matrix  
        ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t: precision parameters
        ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t: dependence parameters
    
    Returns:
        Fitted mean and precision matrix
    """
    n, T = size(Y)
    y_vec = vec(Y)  # Stack columns: [y‚āĀ‚āĀ, y‚āā‚āĀ, ..., y‚āô‚āĀ, y‚āĀ‚āā, ...]
    
    # Precision matrices
    D_s = Diagonal(sum(W_spatial, dims=2)[:])
    D_t = Diagonal(sum(W_temporal, dims=2)[:])
    
    Q_s = ŌĄ_s * (D_s - ŌĀ_s * W_spatial)
    Q_t = ŌĄ_t * (D_t - ŌĀ_t * W_temporal)
    
    # Kronecker precision (spatial ‚äó temporal for column-wise vectorization)
    Q = kron(Q_t, Q_s)
    
    # For intrinsic CAR, add small ridge for numerical stability
    Q += 1e-6 * I
    
    # Compute posterior mean (assuming improper flat prior)
    # For zero-mean process: őľ = 0
    # For regression: őľ = (X'QX)‚ĀĽ¬ĻX'Qy where X is design matrix
    
    # Posterior covariance
    ő£ = inv(Matrix(Q))
    
    # Simulate from posterior (example)
    posterior_samples = rand(MvNormal(zeros(n*T), ő£), 100)
    
    return Q, ő£, posterior_samples
end

function crime_forecasting_example()
    """
    Example: Weekly crime counts across city districts
    """
    # Simulate data
    n_districts = 20
    n_weeks = 52
    
    # Spatial weights (queen contiguity)  
    W_spatial = generate_spatial_weights(n_districts, "queen")
    
    # Temporal weights (first-order random walk)
    W_temporal = generate_temporal_weights(n_weeks, "rw1")
    
    # Parameters
    ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t = 1.0, 2.0
    ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t = 0.8, 0.6
    
    # Generate synthetic crime data with space-time structure
    Y = simulate_spacetime_data(n_districts, n_weeks, W_spatial, W_temporal, 
                               ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t, ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t)
    
    # Add Poisson observation model for counts
    őĽ = exp.(Y .+ 2)  # Log-linear link with intercept
    crime_counts = [rand(Poisson(őĽ[i,t])) for i in 1:n_districts, t in 1:n_weeks]
    
    # Fit model
    Q, ő£, samples = separable_spacetime_car(log.(crime_counts .+ 0.5), 
                                           W_spatial, W_temporal,
                                           ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t, ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t)
    
    return crime_counts, Q, ő£, samples
end

function generate_spatial_weights(n, type="queen") 
    """Generate spatial weights matrix"""
    # Simplified: regular grid with queen contiguity
    grid_size = Int(sqrt(n))
    W = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:grid_size, j in 1:grid_size
        idx = (i-1) * grid_size + j
        # Add neighbors (simplified)
        neighbors = []
        if i > 1; push!(neighbors, (i-2) * grid_size + j); end
        if i < grid_size; push!(neighbors, i * grid_size + j); end  
        if j > 1; push!(neighbors, (i-1) * grid_size + j-1); end
        if j < grid_size; push!(neighbors, (i-1) * grid_size + j+1); end
        
        for nb in neighbors
            if 1 <= nb <= n
                W[idx, nb] = 1
            end
        end
    end
    
    return W
end

function generate_temporal_weights(T, type="rw1")
    """Generate temporal weights matrix"""
    W = zeros(T, T)
    for t in 1:(T-1)
        W[t, t+1] = W[t+1, t] = 1
    end
    return W
end

function simulate_spacetime_data(n, T, W_s, W_t, ŌĄ_s, ŌĄ_t, ŌĀ_s, ŌĀ_t)
    """Simulate from separable space-time CAR"""
    # This is a simplified simulation - full implementation would
    # use proper CAR simulation algorithms
    Y = randn(n, T)
    
    # Apply spatial and temporal smoothing
    for iter in 1:10
        Y_new = copy(Y)
        for i in 1:n, t in 1:T
            spatial_avg = sum(W_s[i, :] .* Y[:, t]) / max(sum(W_s[i, :]), 1)
            temporal_avg = sum(W_t[t, :] .* Y[i, :]) / max(sum(W_t[t, :]), 1)
            Y_new[i, t] = 0.3 * Y[i, t] + 0.4 * spatial_avg + 0.3 * temporal_avg
        end
        Y = Y_new
    end
    
    return Y
end

5.14 Forecasting with Spatial Models

Space-time areal models enable forecasting at multiple horizons, accounting for both spatial contagion and temporal evolution. The forecasting framework must handle the dual uncertainty arising from parameter estimation and future innovations.

5.14.1 One-Step-Ahead Forecasting

For the STAR(1) model, one-step-ahead forecasts at time T+1 given data through time T are:

Ň∑_{T+1|T} = E[y_{T+1} | y_1, ‚Ä¶, y_T] = (I - ŌĀŐā W)^{-1} (ŌÜŐā y_T + X_{T+1} ő≤Őā)

The forecast variance incorporates both parameter uncertainty and innovation variance:

Var(y_{T+1} | Data) = (I - ŌĀŐā W)^{-1} [ŌÉŐā¬≤ I + Var(ŌÜŐā y_T + X_{T+1} ő≤Őā)] (I - ŌĀŐā W)^{-T}

5.14.2 Multi-Step Forecasting

Multi-step forecasts require iterative application of the one-step formula, with forecast uncertainty accumulating over time. For h-step-ahead forecasts:

Ň∑_{T+h|T} = (I - ŌĀŐā W)^{-1} (ŌÜŐā Ň∑_{T+h-1|T} + X_{T+h} ő≤Őā)

The forecast variance grows with horizon h, approaching the unconditional variance for large h in stationary models.

5.14.3 Forecast Model Assessment

Standard forecast evaluation metrics extend to spatial settings:

Mean Absolute Error (MAE): MAE = 1/(nh) ‚ąĎ{i=1}^n ‚ąĎ{t=1}^h |y_{i,T+t} - Ň∑_{i,T+t|T}|

Root Mean Square Error (RMSE): RMSE = ‚ąö{1/(nh) ‚ąĎ_{i
